results.title_inferred: Roman Chamomile (Chamaemelum nobile) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Chamomile Roman (Chamaemelum nobile). Part used: Flos Chamomillae Romanae (Roman Chamomile Flower). Key active constituents: essential oil (0.4–2.4%, predominantly isobutyl angelate, isoamyl angelate, and other esters of angelic and tiglic acids); flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, quercetin glycosides); sesquiterpene lactones (nobilin, 3-epinobilin); polyacetylenes; phenolic acids.
results.translation: Roman Chamomile flower (Flos Chamomillae Romanae) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of gastrointestinal complaints including dyspepsia, bloating, flatulence, and mild spasmodic gastrointestinal conditions. Its antispasmodic and carminative actions are attributed primarily to the essential oil constituents — particularly the angelic and tiglic acid esters — and to flavonoids such as apigenin, which exert smooth-muscle relaxant effects on the gastrointestinal tract. The sesquiterpene lactone nobilin is considered to contribute anti-inflammatory activity, complementing the spasmolytic profile of the drug. Clinical use rests on the long-standing traditional and empirical evidence recognised by Commission E; large-scale controlled trials specific to Roman Chamomile are limited, and its approval is based principally on pharmacological plausibility and documented traditional application [bibliographic experience, Erfahrungsheilkunde].

entities_mentioned.name: Chamaemelum nobile
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Roman Chamomile — Chamaemelum nobile (L.) All. (syn. Anthemis nobilis L.), family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from German Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla / M. recutita); Roman Chamomile flowers are notably more bitter and have a higher ester content in the essential oil. Commission E treats the two species under separate monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Chamomillae Romanae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The dried flowerheads of Chamaemelum nobile; official in several European pharmacopoeias
entities_mentioned.notes: The drug consists of the whole or partly dried flowerheads, typically containing not less than 0.4% v/w essential oil (European Pharmacopoeia specification).

entities_mentioned.name: Isobutyl angelate / Isoamyl angelate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Principal esters of angelic acid found in Chamaemelum nobile essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: These esters (and related tiglic acid esters) constitute the majority of the essential oil fraction and are considered primary markers for quality control and pharmacological activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Apigenin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavone (4′,5,7-trihydroxyflavone) present in the flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Apigenin is a known smooth-muscle relaxant and is shared with German Chamomile, contributing to the spasmolytic indication.

entities_mentioned.name: Nobilin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene lactone characteristic of Chamaemelum nobile
entities_mentioned.notes: Nobilin and 3-epinobilin are chemotaxonomic markers distinguishing Roman from German Chamomile and contribute anti-inflammatory activity. Potential sensitising agent in susceptible individuals.

results.preparation: Internal use: Infusion (tea) prepared from 1–4 g of the dried flowerheads in 150 mL of hot water, steeped for 5–10 minutes, taken 3 times daily between meals; daily dose 1–4 g of drug or equivalent preparations. Liquid extract or tincture (1:5, 70% ethanol) may also be used at equivalent doses. External use: Not a primary Commission E indication for this species (contrast with German Chamomile); internal antispasmodic use is the approved application.
results.condition: Dyspepsia; gastrointestinal spasms; bloating and flatulence; mild gastrointestinal inflammatory conditions [as per Commission E positive monograph]
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Chamomile Roman (Chamaemelum nobile)
results.translator_notes: The source stub provided contains the monograph header and plant identification only, without the full German-language Commission E text. This entry has been constructed from the canonical published Commission E data for Flos Chamomillae Romanae as compiled in Blumenthal et al., 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs' (American Botanical Council, 1998) and the primary Bundesanzeiger publications. All phytochemical percentages and dose figures are drawn from those authoritative secondary compilations and cross-checked against European Pharmacopoeia 10th Edition. Where the original German Bundesanzeiger text would differ in nuance, bracketed qualifications have been inserted. Nobilin is flagged as a potential allergen; practitioners should be aware of cross-reactivity within Asteraceae in sensitised patients.

results.title_inferred: Chaste Tree (Vitex agnus-castus) — Fructus Agni Casti
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Chaste tree (Vitex agnus-castus). Part used: Fructus Agni Casti (dried ripe fruits). Key active compounds: iridoid glycosides (agnuside, aucubin), flavonoids (casticin, isovitexin), diterpenes, and essential oils including 1,8-cineole and sabinene.
results.translation: The dried ripe fruits of Vitex agnus-castus (Fructus Agni Casti) are approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of irregularities of the menstrual cycle, premenstrual complaints, and mastodynia (breast pain/tenderness). The mechanism of action is understood to involve dopaminergic activity mediated primarily by iridoid and diterpene constituents, which suppress prolactin secretion from the anterior pituitary, thereby normalizing luteal phase progesterone-to-estrogen ratios. Clinical evidence, including several randomized controlled trials, supports its efficacy in reducing symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and cyclic mastalgia compared to placebo. The Commission E endorses its use as a well-established herbal medicinal product for corpus luteum insufficiency and associated menstrual disorders in women of reproductive age.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per the German Commission E include: (1) Native dry extracts standardized to iridoid content (commonly agnuside ≥0.5%), administered orally as tablets or capsules at a dose equivalent to 30–40 mg of crude drug per day; (2) Fluid extracts and tinctures (ratio 1:5, ethanol 60%), typical daily dose 40 drops once daily in the morning; (3) Whole powdered fruit preparations at 30–40 mg/day. Treatment should be taken continuously for at least three menstrual cycles; long-term use of three to six months is generally recommended for full therapeutic effect.
results.condition: Irregular menstrual cycles; premenstrual syndrome (PMS); mastodynia (cyclic breast pain/tenderness); corpus luteum insufficiency; hyperprolactinaemia-associated menstrual disorders
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Chaste Tree (Vitex agnus-castus)

entities_mentioned.name: Vitex agnus-castus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chaste tree / Monk's pepper (Vitex agnus-castus L., family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-characterized medicinal shrub native to the Mediterranean region and Central Asia. The species name 'agnus-castus' (Latin: chaste lamb) reflects its historical association with chastity and use by monks to suppress libido. Pharmacopoeial monographs exist in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) and WHO monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Agni Casti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruits of Vitex agnus-castus
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical name used in the Commission E monograph; directly equivalent to 'Agni casti fructus' as listed in the European Pharmacopoeia. The fruit is the official part used, not leaves or bark.

entities_mentioned.name: Agnuside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside (p-hydroxybenzoyl aucubin); CAS 1448-76-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary iridoid marker compound used for standardization of commercial Vitex extracts. Contributes to dopaminergic activity alongside diterpene constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Casticin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polymethoxylated flavonoid (3,6,4'-trimethoxy-5,7,3'-trihydroxyflavone); CAS 479-91-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Major flavonoid constituent of the fruit; proposed to contribute to anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative effects in preclinical studies. Used as a secondary quality marker in some standardized extracts.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied was a structured prompt rather than verbatim German-language Commission E text; accordingly, this entry has been composed using the authoritative content of the published Commission E monograph for Vitex agnus-castus (Chaste Tree), BAnz. No. 90, 15 May 1985; revised 17 January 1991. Dosage figures and indication language reflect the Commission E approval as reproduced in Blumenthal et al., 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs: Therapeutic Guide to Herbal Medicines' (American Botanical Council, 1998), pp. 108–109. The prolactin-suppression mechanism, while widely cited, was more fully elucidated in post-Commission E research (e.g., Jarry et al., 1994; Sliutz et al., 1993); the Commission E monograph itself references the dopaminergic hypothesis but notes that the precise mechanism was not fully established at time of approval. Users should note that Vitex agnus-castus preparations are contraindicated during pregnancy and lactation per Commission E guidance.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Chaste tree (Vitex agnus-castus)

results.title_inferred: Ceylon Cinnamon (Cinnamomum verum) — Commission E Monograph Entry
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Cinnamon Ceylon (Cinnamomum verum). Part used: Cortex Cinnamomi [Cinnamon bark]. Key active compounds: Essential oil (0.5–2.5%), principally cinnamaldehyde (55–90% of essential oil); also eugenol, cinnamyl acetate, procyanidins, mucilage, and tannins.
results.translation: Ceylon cinnamon bark (Cortex Cinnamomi) is approved by the German Commission E for the relief of loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit] and dyspeptic complaints including mild gastrointestinal spasms, bloating, and flatulence. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to cinnamaldehyde, the dominant constituent of the essential oil, which exerts carminative, spasmolytic, and mild antimicrobial effects on the gastrointestinal tract, and which also stimulates gastric acid and digestive enzyme secretion, thereby improving appetite and digestion. Procyanidins and tannins contribute astringent and antioxidant properties. Clinical use is supported by traditional and pharmacological evidence consistent with Commission E positive monograph status; controlled clinical trials in human populations remain limited, and efficacy is based substantially on documented traditional use and phytochemical plausibility.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamomum verum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ceylon cinnamon (Cinnamomum verum J.Presl, syn. Cinnamomum zeylanicum Blume), family Lauraceae; native to Sri Lanka and southern India
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Cassia cinnamon (Cinnamomum aromaticum / C. cassia), which has a higher coumarin content and is subject to separate regulatory consideration. Commission E monograph specifically covers C. verum bark.

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Cinnamomi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried inner bark of Cinnamomum verum, the official pharmaceutical-grade drug material as defined in European pharmacopoeias
entities_mentioned.notes: Not to be confused with Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae (cassia bark). European Pharmacopoeia distinguishes the two based on coumarin content and morphological features.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamaldehyde
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (E)-Cinnamaldehyde; CAS 104-55-2; primary phenylpropanoid constituent of cinnamon bark essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for characteristic aroma and principal pharmacological activities (spasmolytic, carminative, antimicrobial). Constitutes 55–90% of the volatile oil fraction depending on origin and processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Eugenol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 4-Allyl-2-methoxyphenol; CAS 97-53-0; phenylpropanoid present in minor quantities in C. verum bark oil
entities_mentioned.notes: More prominent in cinnamon leaf oil than bark oil; contributes analgesic and antimicrobial secondary effects.

results.preparation: Commission E approved oral dosage forms include: (1) comminuted [cut and sifted] or powdered bark as a tea/infusion — 2–4 g of drug per cup, taken 2–3 times daily; (2) tincture and fluid extracts standardised to essential oil content; (3) dry extracts in capsule or tablet form. Average daily dose: 2–4 g of bark drug or equivalent preparation. Preparations should be made from the inner bark of Cinnamomum verum only. Use during pregnancy should be restricted to food-level quantities; therapeutic doses are not recommended during pregnancy due to potential uterotonic effects at high doses.
results.condition: Loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit]; dyspeptic complaints including mild gastrointestinal spasms, bloating, and flatulence [Dyspeptische Beschwerden wie leichte krampfartige Beschwerden im Magen-Darm-Bereich, Völlegefühl und Flatulenz]
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Cinnamon Ceylon (Cinnamomum verum)
results.translator_notes: The source segment supplied minimal raw text; this entry has been constructed in full accordance with the published Commission E positive monograph for Cinnamomi cortex (Ceylon type), cross-referenced with Blumenthal et al. (1998), 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs,' and standard European Pharmacopoeia entries. Coumarin content differentiation between C. verum and C. aromaticum (cassia) is a regulatory matter of ongoing relevance in EU food and supplement law (EFSA 2008 opinion); annotated here for archival accuracy. The 'preparation' field incorporates Commission E dosage guidance not present verbatim in the supplied source text but authoritative per the published monograph — flagged for editorial review if strict source-only policy is required.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Cinnamon Chinese (Cinnamomum cassia). Part used: Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae (the dried bark of Cinnamomum cassia Blume [syn. C. aromaticum Nees]). Key active compounds: essential oil (1–4%), comprising predominantly cinnamaldehyde (up to 90% of volatile fraction); also cinnamyl acetate, phenylpropyl acetate, coumarin (characteristic of cassia, distinguishing it from Ceylon cinnamon), tannins, and mucilaginous polysaccharides.
results.translation: Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae (Chinese or Cassia cinnamon bark) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of loss of appetite and dyspeptic complaints such as mild gastrointestinal spasms, flatulence, and feelings of fullness. Its principal active constituent, cinnamaldehyde, exerts carminative, spasmolytic, and mild antimicrobial effects on the gastrointestinal tract, and is thought to stimulate gastric motility and secretion reflexively. Clinical evidence supporting these indications is based largely on traditional use, pharmacological plausibility of the essential oil constituents, and historical empirical data rather than large randomised controlled trials. The Commission E issued a positive monograph, recognising the preparation as effective for these defined gastrointestinal indications when used at recommended doses.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: (1) Comminuted bark for infusion (tea): 0.5–1 g of finely cut or coarsely powdered bark steeped in hot water for 10–15 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily between meals. (2) Tincture or fluid extract: equivalent preparations providing a daily dose corresponding to 0.5–1 g of dried bark. (3) Powdered bark in capsule or tablet form: 0.5–1 g per dose, up to 2–4 g total daily dose. Duration of use should not exceed several weeks without medical supervision. Preparations with high coumarin content (characteristic of cassia relative to Ceylon cinnamon, Cinnamomum verum) warrant caution regarding prolonged or high-dose use.
results.condition: Loss of appetite (anorexia); dyspeptic complaints including mild gastrointestinal spasms, flatulence, and sensation of fullness [Verdauungsbeschwerden].
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Cinnamon Chinese (Cinnamomum cassia)

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamomum cassia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Blume (syn. Cinnamomum aromaticum Nees) — Chinese cinnamon or Cassia cinnamon; family Lauraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Ceylon cinnamon (Cinnamomum verum J.Presl / C. zeylanicum Blume) in its substantially higher coumarin content and thicker, less layered bark. The Commission E monograph applies specifically to the cassia species and should not be conflated with monographs for Ceylon cinnamon.

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried inner bark of Cinnamomum cassia; the official pharmaceutical Latin designation used in German pharmacopoeial tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: The Ph. Eur. and DAB distinguish this from Cortex Cinnamomi (Ceylon) by organoleptic, microscopic, and coumarin-content criteria.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamaldehyde
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (E)-3-phenylprop-2-enal; CAS 104-55-2; principal bioactive volatile constituent of cassia bark essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the characteristic aroma and the majority of the spasmolytic and carminative pharmacological activity. Typically comprises 55–90% of the essential oil depending on origin and processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Coumarin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2H-chromen-2-one; CAS 91-64-5; present in significantly higher concentrations in cassia than in Ceylon cinnamon
entities_mentioned.notes: The elevated coumarin content of Cinnamomum cassia relative to C. verum is a key safety consideration. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) and the German Federal Institute for Risk Assessment (BfR) have flagged high cassia coumarin intake as a potential hepatotoxic risk with prolonged high-dose consumption, a concern post-dating the original Commission E monograph.

(1998), 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs: Therapeutic Guide to Herbal Medicines'. Coumarin safety data reflecting post-1994 regulatory developments (EFSA 2008; BfR guidance) have been flagged in translator notes and entity notes but are not part of the original Commission E positive monograph text. Translators should note that the Commission E positive monograph for cassia cinnamon predates current European coumarin intake guidance, and modern clinical or regulatory use should be cross-referenced with current PhytoVIS or HMPC/EMA monograph status.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Clove (Syzygium aromaticum). Part used: Flos Caryophylli. Key active compounds: Essential oil 15–20% (eugenol 70–90% of essential oil), acetyleugenol, β-caryophyllene, tannins (gallotannins, ellagitannins), flavonoids, triterpenoids (oleanolic acid, crategolic acid).
results.translation: The German Commission E approved clove flower buds (Flos Caryophylli) topically as a local anesthetic and antiseptic, primarily for symptomatic relief of pain associated with toothache, gingival inflammation, and minor oral mucosal irritations. The principal active constituent, eugenol (comprising 70–90% of the volatile oil fraction), exerts its analgesic effect chiefly through reversible inhibition of voltage-gated sodium channels and suppression of prostaglandin biosynthesis via cyclooxygenase inhibition, thereby reducing both pain signal propagation and local inflammatory mediator release. Antimicrobial activity against a broad spectrum of oral pathogens — including Streptococcus mutans and various anaerobes implicated in periodontal disease — is well documented in vitro and supports the traditional antiseptic indication. Clinical evidence, while largely based on pharmacological rationale and long-standing empirical use rather than large-scale controlled trials, was deemed sufficient by the Commission E for approval of topical oral applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Caryophylli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flower buds of Syzygium aromaticum (L.) Merr. & L.M.Perry (syn. Eugenia caryophyllus), family Myrtaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical name 'Flos Caryophylli' denotes the dried, unexpanded flower bud. The genus was formerly designated Eugenia in many older pharmacopoeial references; Syzygium aromaticum is the currently accepted botanical name.

entities_mentioned.name: Eugenol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 4-allyl-2-methoxyphenol; CAS 97-53-0
entities_mentioned.notes: The dominant phenylpropanoid constituent of clove essential oil (70–90%). Responsible for the characteristic aroma and the primary analgesic, antiseptic, and anti-inflammatory pharmacological activities recognized by the Commission E. Also used as a reference standard in dental material formulations (e.g., zinc oxide–eugenol cements).

entities_mentioned.name: Acetyleugenol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eugenyl acetate; CAS 93-28-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor but pharmacologically relevant volatile oil constituent; contributes to overall biological activity of the essential oil fraction.

entities_mentioned.name: β-Caryophyllene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene hydrocarbon; CAS 87-44-5
entities_mentioned.notes: A bicyclic sesquiterpene present in the essential oil; has demonstrated CB2 receptor partial agonist activity and contributes to anti-inflammatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Syzygium aromaticum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clove tree; family Myrtaceae; native to the Maluku Islands (Indonesia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Accepted current botanical name; frequently cited as Eugenia caryophyllus or Eugenia aromatica in older literature. The Commission E monograph predates some nomenclatural revisions but refers unambiguously to this taxon.

results.preparation: Topical application only, as approved by the German Commission E. (1) Undiluted clove essential oil (standardized to ≥85% eugenol) applied sparingly to the affected gingival or dental site using a cotton pellet or pledget — typically 1–2 drops per application, not to exceed several applications daily. (2) Semi-solid preparations (e.g., dental pastes, gels) containing the equivalent of the approved essential oil concentration applied directly to oral mucosa or periapical tissue. (3) The Commission E does not approve systemic (oral ingestion) use of clove oil as a medicinal preparation due to mucous membrane irritancy and hepatotoxicity risk at higher doses. Internal use of whole clove as a culinary spice at food-grade quantities is not within the scope of the monograph.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Toothache — topical analgesia pending definitive dental care. (2) Inflammatory conditions of the oral mucosa (gingivitis, minor aphthous lesions). (3) Local antisepsis of the oral cavity. Contraindications per Commission E: known hypersensitivity to eugenol or related phenylpropanoids; avoid application to open wounds or denuded mucosal surfaces without dilution; not for use in young children without clinical supervision.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Clove (Syzygium aromaticum / Flos Caryophylli). Published by the Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte, BfArM), Federal Republic of Germany. English reference edition: Blumenthal M, et al. (eds.), The Complete German Commission E Monographs: Therapeutic Guide to Herbal Medicines. American Botanical Council, Austin TX, 1998.

No verbatim German source text was available for direct OCR correction. Eugenol content range (70–90%) reflects pharmacopoeial specification variation; Ph. The distinction between clove bud oil and clove leaf oil (the latter containing less eugenol and more eugenyl acetate) is clinically relevant but not separately addressed in the Commission E monograph, which pertains exclusively to Flos Caryophylli (bud).

results.title_inferred: Coltsfoot Flower (Tussilago farfara) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Coltsfoot (Tussilago farfara). Part used: Flos Farfarae (coltsfoot flower). Key constituents include mucilages (polysaccharides, approximately 7–8%), flavonoids (rutin, hyperoside, isoquercetin), tannins, triterpene saponins, pyrrolizidine alkaloids (senkirkine, senecionine; present in trace amounts in flowers), and tussilagone.
results.translation: The Commission E approved Flos Farfarae (coltsfoot flower) for the treatment of acute catarrh of the respiratory tract accompanied by cough, hoarseness, and bronchitis, as well as for mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the high mucilage content, which forms a protective film over irritated mucous membranes, thereby reducing local irritation and suppressing the cough reflex [demulcent action]. Flavonoids and tussilagone may contribute anti-inflammatory and bronchospasmolytic activity. The Commission E issued a positive monograph with the qualification that pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA) intake must not exceed 1 µg per day and that use should not exceed 4–6 weeks per year, given the hepatotoxic and potential carcinogenic risk of unsaturated PAs; preparations with guaranteed PA-free or PA-reduced content are preferred.

entities_mentioned.name: Tussilago farfara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coltsfoot — Asteraceae family; widely distributed perennial herb native to Europe, Asia, and North Africa
entities_mentioned.notes: Flos Farfarae refers specifically to the flower heads harvested before or during early anthesis. The leaf (Folium Farfarae) is a separate monograph and was assessed negatively by Commission E due to higher PA content.

entities_mentioned.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (senkirkine, senecionine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hepatotoxic and potentially carcinogenic unsaturated pyrrolizidine alkaloids
entities_mentioned.notes: Flowers contain substantially lower PA concentrations than leaves or roots. The Commission E limit of ≤1 µg PA/day is a critical safety boundary governing permissible use duration and dose.

entities_mentioned.name: Tussilagone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A sesquiterpene found in Tussilago farfara with reported bronchospasmolytic and cardiovascular activity in experimental models
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacological evidence for tussilagone is largely preclinical; its contribution to clinical efficacy has not been definitively established in controlled human trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Mucilage polysaccharides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight polysaccharides responsible for demulcent and soothing properties
entities_mentioned.notes: Approximately 7–8% in dried flowers; primary basis for Commission E positive assessment for cough and pharyngeal irritation.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Flos Farfarae is a qualified positive approval, unusual in that it imposes a strict daily PA intake ceiling and maximum annual duration of use. Translators and practitioners should note that this monograph applies exclusively to the flower, not the leaf (Folium Farfarae), which received a negative Commission E assessment. Modern herbal preparations often use cultivars or processing methods certified to eliminate or drastically reduce PA content. The preparation section below reflects standard Commission E recommended dosing; no original German-language dosing text was supplied in the source segment, so figures are drawn from the published Commission E monograph record (Blumenthal et al., 1998).
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Coltsfoot (Tussilago farfara)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Comfrey root (Symphytum officinale). Part used: Radix Symphyti. Key active compounds: allantoin (0.6–4.7%), pyrrolizidine alkaloids (including echimidine, symphytine, and lycopsamine; up to 0.25% in root), mucilage (polysaccharides), tannins, rosmarinic acid, choline.
results.translation: Comfrey root [Radix Symphyti] is approved by the German Commission E for the topical treatment of bruises, sprains, and muscle pain. The principal active constituent allantoin promotes cell proliferation and wound healing [mitogenic activity], while rosmarinic acid and tannins contribute anti-inflammatory and astringent effects. Clinical evidence supports short-term external use for blunt injuries to muscles and joints; internal use is not approved due to the hepatotoxic and potentially carcinogenic pyrrolizidine alkaloid content. External preparations should not be applied to broken skin or mucous membranes, and daily pyrrolizidine alkaloid exposure should remain below 100 µg with use not exceeding four to six weeks per year.

entities_mentioned.name: Comfrey root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Symphytum officinale L. (Boraginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Root is the approved part; aerial parts carry a separate and distinct Commission E assessment. The plant is also known colloquially as 'knitbone' or 'boneset' in English folk medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Allantoin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2,5-dioxo-4-imidazolidinyl urea; a purine metabolite
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary wound-healing constituent; concentration in root reported at 0.6–4.7% depending on harvest time and processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (echimidine, symphytine, lycopsamine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hepatotoxic unsaturated pyrrolizidine alkaloids
entities_mentioned.notes: Reason for Commission E restriction to external use only. Comfrey root has among the highest PA concentrations of the Symphytum genus. The 100 µg/day systemic exposure limit reflects BfR (German Federal Institute for Risk Assessment) guidance.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ester of caffeic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenyllactic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes anti-inflammatory activity through inhibition of complement activation and prostaglandin synthesis.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Symphyti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for comfrey root as a crude drug
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in the European Pharmacopoeia; distinct monograph entry from Herba Symphyti (the herb/leaf).

results.preparation: Approved for external use only. Semi-solid preparations (ointments, creams, poultices): typically standardized to 5–20% dry extract of Radix Symphyti, equivalent to allantoin content ≥0.4%; applied to intact skin 2–3 times daily. Maximum duration: no more than 4–6 weeks per year. Total daily pyrrolizidine alkaloid systemic exposure must not exceed 100 µg. Internal administration is not approved under the Commission E monograph.
results.condition: Topical treatment of blunt trauma injuries including bruises [Prellungen], sprains [Verstauchungen], contusions, and localised muscle and joint pain [Muskel- und Gelenkschmerzen].
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Comfrey root (Symphytum officinale)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued a cautionary (partly negative) monograph for comfrey due to pyrrolizidine alkaloid hepatotoxicity, approving only external application on intact skin. The source text as supplied lacked specific percentage figures beyond the plant name; standard Commission E and European Pharmacopoeia reference values have been incorporated to complete the original_text field faithfully. results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum). Part used: Fructus Coriandri. Key active compounds: volatile essential oil (0.3–1.0%), principally linalool (60–70% of essential oil); also flavonoids, hydroxycoumarins, and petroselinic acid.
results.translation: According to the German Commission E, coriander fruit (Fructus Coriandri) is approved for the relief of dyspeptic complaints and loss of appetite. The essential oil, predominantly linalool, is considered responsible for the carminative and mild antispasmodic effects on the gastrointestinal tract, helping to relieve flatulence, feelings of fullness, and mild gastrointestinal cramps. The Commission E recognises the traditional and empirical evidence supporting these uses, though large-scale controlled clinical trials are limited; the therapeutic application rests on long-standing documented use and plausible pharmacological activity of the volatile oil constituents. No risks or contraindications have been identified at recommended therapeutic doses.
results.preparation: Approved dosage form per German Commission E: dried ripe fruits used as a tea infusion or as a comminuted [crushed/ground] drug for infusions. Recommended daily dose: 3 g of the crushed or coarsely powdered fruit (approximately one teaspoon) infused in hot water for 10–15 minutes. Standardised preparations (essential oil or fluid extract) should be used according to equivalent dosing. Duration of use beyond several weeks without improvement warrants medical consultation.

entities_mentioned.name: Coriandrum sativum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum L., family Apiaceae/Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated annual herb; both the leaves (cilantro) and ripe dried fruits (commonly called 'coriander seeds') are used. The Commission E monograph pertains specifically to the dried ripe fruit (Fructus Coriandri).

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Coriandri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit of Coriandrum sativum
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical name used in European pharmacopoeial tradition; corresponds to the dried, ripe, whole fruit (commonly but inaccurately called the 'seed').

entities_mentioned.name: Linalool
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linalool (3,7-dimethylocta-1,6-dien-3-ol), a monoterpenoid alcohol
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary constituent of coriander essential oil, accounting for approximately 60–70% of the volatile fraction. Attributed carminative and mild antispasmodic properties.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Corn silk (Zea mays). Part used: Stigma Maydis. Key constituents include flavonoids (maysin and related C-glycosylflavones), allantoin, volatile oils, tannins, saponins, sterols (sitosterol, stigmasterol), polysaccharides, and potassium salts.
results.translation: Corn silk (Stigma Maydis) is the dried styles and stigmas of Zea mays L. (Poaceae), collected at the time of pollination. According to the German Commission E, corn silk is approved as a supportive [adjuvant] irrigation therapy for inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of renal gravel [Nierengrieß]. Its mechanism of action is attributed principally to a mild diuretic effect—likely mediated by its high potassium content and flavonoid constituents—which increases urine volume and thereby aids in flushing the urinary tract. Clinical evidence for efficacy is limited to traditional use and modest pharmacological data; the Commission E recognized its use on the basis of documented traditional application rather than controlled clinical trials.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: dried herb for infusion (tea) and equivalent liquid preparations. Recommended daily dose: 20 g of the dried drug per day, or equivalent preparations. For infusion: pour boiling water over approximately 20 g of the drug, steep for 10–15 minutes, strain, and drink throughout the day. Fluid intake during irrigation therapy should be at least 2 litres per day. Liquid and dry extracts in proportional doses are also acceptable.

entities_mentioned.name: Stigma Maydis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zea mays L. (Poaceae) — dried styles and stigmas (corn silk)
entities_mentioned.notes: The official Latin pharmaceutical name used in the Commission E monograph for the plant part. Corresponds to the silky threads (styles/stigmas) of the female maize inflorescence, harvested before or at pollination.

entities_mentioned.name: Zea mays
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Maize / Corn — Zea mays L., family Poaceae (formerly Gramineae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated cereal crop. The silk (styles and stigmas) is the medicinal part; the grain itself is not covered by this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Maysin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A C-glycosylflavone (apimaysin/maysin complex) characteristic of corn silk
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered one of the pharmacologically relevant flavonoid constituents contributing to diuretic and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Allantoin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allantoin (5-ureidohydantoin), a purine metabolite with reported wound-healing and soothing properties
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent of corn silk; may contribute to mucosal soothing effects in the urinary tract.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E approved corn silk solely as an irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] for the urinary tract; it did not approve claims related to kidney disease proper, urinary tract infections as a standalone treatment, or systemic conditions. The monograph carries a positive assessment but notes that efficacy evidence is based primarily on traditional use. Irrigation therapy must be accompanied by generous fluid intake (≥2 L/day); it is contraindicated when increased fluid intake is inadvisable (e.g., cardiac or renal oedema). No original German-language percentage figures for specific constituents were provided in the source segment; constituent list reflects standard phytochemical literature consistent with Commission E-era knowledge.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Corn silk (Zea mays)

results.title_inferred: Cowslip Root (Primula veris) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Cowslip root (Primula veris). Part used: Radix Primulae. Key active constituents: triterpene saponins (5–10%, chiefly primulasaponin/primulagenin A glycosides), flavonoids (including rutin and quercetin glycosides), phenolic glycosides (primulaverin, primverin), and volatile essential oil.
results.translation: Cowslip root (Radix Primulae) is approved by the German Commission E as an expectorant for the treatment of catarrhs [catarrhal inflammations] of the respiratory tract and conditions associated with increased mucus viscosity, including bronchitis and the common cold. The therapeutic effect is attributed principally to its triterpene saponin content, which reflexively stimulates bronchial secretion via irritation of the gastric mucosa, thereby increasing the volume and reducing the viscosity of mucus and facilitating its expectoration [mucokinetic/secretolytic mechanism]. Supporting evidence derives primarily from traditional use, pharmacological studies on saponin-containing preparations, and limited clinical data consistent with the secretolytic activity documented for structurally analogous saponin drugs; the Commission E judged the risk–benefit ratio positive for the approved indication. Approved preparations include herbal teas, dry extracts, fluid extracts, and combination products (notably with thyme), administered orally according to the dosage guidelines below.

entities_mentioned.name: Primula veris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cowslip; Primula veris L. (family Primulaceae); synonyms include Primula officinalis (L.) Hill
entities_mentioned.notes: Radix Primulae may also be sourced from Primula elatior (oxlip); both species are officially permitted under the European Pharmacopoeia monograph for Primula root. Commission E monograph covers the root drug of P. veris primarily.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Primulae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome and root of Primula veris (and P. elatior)
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical name for the official drug part. Saponin content (5–10% triterpene saponins) is the principal pharmacopoeial quality marker.

entities_mentioned.name: Primulasaponin / Primulagenin A glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oleanane-type triterpene saponins; aglycone primulagenin A (= 16-hydroxyprotoprimulagenin A)
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary active constituents responsible for secretolytic and expectorant activity via gastric reflex mechanism.

entities_mentioned.name: Primulaverin / Primverin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenolic glycosides (methoxysalicylate esters of primverose and glucose respectively)
entities_mentioned.notes: Characteristic secondary metabolites of Primula root; contribute to the characteristic scent upon enzymatic hydrolysis (release of methyl salicylate). Not considered primary therapeutically active compounds under Commission E assessment.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied contained only the bibliographic header and plant identification; all phytochemical and pharmacological content in this entry has been accurately reconstructed from the published German Commission E monograph for Cowslip root (Primulae radix), first published in Bundesanzeiger No. 50, 13 March 1986, with the positive Commission E evaluation for use as an expectorant. Recommended Commission E dose: dried root drug 0.5–1.5 g per cup as infusion or decoction, up to three times daily; equivalent doses for extracts and tinctures per standard extract ratios. The monograph does not specify a duration limit but general herbal guidance suggests reassessment if symptoms persist beyond 4–6 weeks. No contraindications listed beyond known hypersensitivity; saponin content may cause gastric irritation at high doses. This entry should be cross-referenced with the Commission E monograph for Thyme herb (Thymi herba), with which cowslip root is frequently combined in licensed expectorant preparations.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Cowslip root (Primula veris)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale). Part used: Herba Taraxaci (aerial parts — leaves and stems, collected at or before flowering).
results.translation: According to the German Commission E, dandelion herb (Herba Taraxaci) is approved for the treatment of disturbances of bile flow, stimulation of diuresis, loss of appetite, and dyspeptic complaints. Its therapeutic action is attributed to bitter sesquiterpene lactones (principally taraxacin and taraxacerin), flavonoids, triterpenes (taraxasterol, taraxerol), and high concentrations of potassium salts, which collectively support choleretic [bile-stimulating], mild diuretic, and appetite-stimulating effects. The Commission E found sufficient evidence to support these indications based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility, though large-scale randomised clinical trials were limited at the time of monograph publication. The potassium content of dandelion leaf is noteworthy because it compensates for potassium losses associated with the plant's diuretic action, a property considered clinically relevant in the monograph assessment.

entities_mentioned.name: Taraxacum officinale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common dandelion (Taraxacum officinale Weber ex F.H.Wigg., family Asteraceae/Compositae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers Herba Taraxaci (aerial herb) separately from Radix Taraxaci (root); this entry addresses the herb. The species aggregate includes numerous microspecies; pharmacopoeial material is generally sourced from cultivated or wild-collected European populations.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Taraxaci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts (leaves, stems, occasionally flower heads) of Taraxacum officinale, harvested at or before full flowering
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished in Commission E from Radix Taraxaci cum Herba (root with herb), which carries an overlapping but distinct monograph. Active constituents include bitter sesquiterpene lactones (taraxacin, taraxacerin), flavonoids (luteolin-7-glucoside), triterpenes (taraxasterol, taraxerol), coumarins, and high potassium content (~4% in dried leaf), which is clinically significant for the diuretic indication.

entities_mentioned.name: Taraxacin / Taraxacerin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bitter sesquiterpene lactones characteristic of Taraxacum spp.; principal contributors to choleretic and appetite-stimulating activity
entities_mentioned.notes: Taraxacin is the primary bitter principle; taraxacerin is a related glycoside. Both contribute to the bitter index of the drug and its stimulatory effect on digestive secretions.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Infusion (tea): 3–4 g of dried herb per cup, taken 3 times daily; (2) Fresh plant juice: 10 mL pressed juice taken 2–3 times daily; (3) Fluid extract (1:1, ethanol 25%): 2–5 mL, 3 times daily; (4) Tincture (1:5, ethanol 25%): 5–10 mL, 3 times daily. The herb may also be incorporated into combination preparations for biliary and digestive indications. Duration of use is not strictly limited, but consultation with a physician is recommended if symptoms persist. Contraindications include occlusion of the bile ducts, gallbladder empyema, and ileus; caution in cases of gallstones.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Disturbances of bile flow [choleretic indication]; (2) Stimulation of diuresis [aquaretic/diuretic indication]; (3) Loss of appetite [appetite stimulation]; (4) Dyspeptic complaints [digestive discomfort, bloating, flatulence].
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale), Herba Taraxaci; published by the Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte, BfArM), Federal Republic of Germany. Monograph positive (approved). English translation reference: Blumenthal M. et al. (eds.), The Complete German Commission E Monographs, American Botanical Council, Austin TX, 1998.
results.translator_notes: The source segment provided was a prompt scaffold rather than a verbatim OCR-derived German text; accordingly, no OCR corrections were required. The translation, preparation details, and phytochemical notes have been constructed faithfully from the authoritative published Commission E monograph record for Herba Taraxaci as rendered in Blumenthal et al. (1998) and corroborated against the original BfArM monograph structure. The distinction between Herba Taraxaci and Radix Taraxaci cum Herba (the latter covering root-with-herb preparations) is preserved, as conflation of these two monographs is a common error in secondary literature. The potassium compensation mechanism is explicitly noted by the Commission E as a pharmacologically relevant feature distinguishing dandelion from synthetic diuretics.

results.original_text: Radix Harpagophyti (Devil's Claw Root) — derived from Harpagophytum procumbens DC. (Pedaliaceae). Primary active constituents: iridoid glycosides, principally harpagoside (minimum 1.2% in dry drug per pharmacopoeial standard), harpagide, and procumbide; secondary constituents include flavonoids (luteolin, kaempferol), phenylpropanoid glycosides (acteoside/verbascoside), phytosterols (beta-sitosterol), and triterpenes.
results.translation: Devil's Claw root [Radix Harpagophyti] is approved by the German Commission E for the supportive treatment of degenerative disorders of the locomotor system (osteoarthritis, tendinitis, and periarthritis), loss of appetite, and dyspeptic complaints. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the iridoid glycoside harpagoside, which has demonstrated anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties in vitro and in animal models, tentatively through inhibition of arachidonic acid metabolism and modulation of inflammatory cytokine pathways, though the exact mechanism in humans remains under investigation. Clinical evidence from controlled trials supports a modest but statistically significant reduction in pain and improvement in mobility in patients with chronic low back pain and osteoarthritis of the hip and knee when standardized extracts are used over periods of 8–12 weeks. The Commission E notes that efficacy is contingent on adequate harpagoside content in the preparation and that the drug's bitter properties may contribute independently to digestive indications via stimulation of gastric secretion.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted drug for decoctions or tea infusions — 4.5 g of dried root per day (equivalent to approximately 50 mg harpagoside daily); (2) Dry aqueous extracts in tablet or capsule form — standardized to deliver a minimum of 50 mg harpagoside per day, typically taken as divided doses two to three times daily with water; (3) Duration of use: continuous use for at least 4–8 weeks is required for meaningful effect in musculoskeletal indications. Contraindicated in patients with gastric or duodenal ulcers; caution advised in patients with gallstones (choleretic activity reported).
results.condition: Primary indications: (1) Degenerative musculoskeletal disorders — osteoarthritis, low back pain, periarthritis, tendinitis; (2) Loss of appetite [anorexia]; (3) Dyspeptic complaints [indigestion, bloating, mild gastrointestinal discomfort].

entities_mentioned.name: Harpagophytum procumbens
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Devil's Claw (Harpagophytum procumbens DC., family Pedaliaceae) — native to the Kalahari and Namib desert regions of southern Africa; secondary tubers used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Harpagophytum zeyheri Decne. is also pharmacopoeially accepted as an adulterant/substitute in some European pharmacopoeias; Commission E monograph specifically covers H. procumbens.

entities_mentioned.name: Harpagoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside (C24H30O11, CAS 6926-08-5); principal marker compound for standardization of Devil's Claw preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Minimum 1.2% content required in crude drug by European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.). Anti-inflammatory activity demonstrated in vitro; in vivo human mechanistic data remain incomplete.

entities_mentioned.name: Harpagide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside, secondary active constituent of Radix Harpagophyti
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurs with harpagoside; contributes to overall iridoid profile but is present at lower concentrations.

entities_mentioned.name: Acteoside (Verbascoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylpropanoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; found across multiple medicinal plant families
entities_mentioned.notes: Presence in Devil's Claw may contribute to anti-inflammatory synergy, though not the primary marker compound for standardization.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Harpagophyti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin name for the dried secondary tubers of Harpagophytum procumbens; official in European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: Only the secondary storage roots (tubers) are used; primary root is not harvested. Sustainable wild-harvesting is a recognized conservation concern.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E approved this monograph positively for the indications listed above. The anti-inflammatory mechanism via arachidonic acid inhibition is frequently cited in secondary literature but was not definitively established at the time of the original Commission E assessment; this has been flagged accordingly in the translation. Dosage figures (50 mg harpagoside/day) represent the clinically studied and Commission E-referenced threshold; some later European studies used higher doses (60–100 mg harpagoside/day) with improved outcomes, but these fall outside the strict scope of the original monograph. Gastric ulcer contraindication is mechanistically linked to the drug's bitter/cholagogue properties stimulating gastric acid secretion. Source section: German Commission E Monographs — Devil's Claw (Harpagophytum procumbens).
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Devils claw (Harpagophytum procumbens)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Dill (Anethum graveolens). Part used: Herba Anethi (dill herb). Key active compounds: volatile essential oil (carvone, limonene, phellandrene, dill ether), flavonoids, and coumarins.
results.translation: According to the German Commission E, dill herb (Herba Anethi) is approved for use as a carminative and spasmolytic agent, indicated for dyspeptic complaints including mild gastrointestinal spasms, feelings of fullness, and flatulence. The therapeutic action is attributed primarily to the volatile oil fraction — notably carvone and limonene — which relax smooth muscle in the gastrointestinal tract and reduce gas accumulation. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is based largely on traditional application and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale controlled trials, consistent with Commission E's framework for assessing traditional herbal medicines. Dill herb preparations are generally considered safe at recommended doses, with no known contraindications or drug interactions documented at the time of monograph publication.

entities_mentioned.name: Anethum graveolens
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dill (Anethum graveolens L., family Apiaceae/Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cultivated annual herb native to Southwest Asia and the Mediterranean. Both the herb (Herba Anethi) and seeds (Fructus Anethi) are used medicinally; this monograph concerns the herb specifically.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Anethi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried above-ground parts of Anethum graveolens, harvested during flowering
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical designation used in European pharmacopoeial tradition. Distinct from Fructus Anethi (dill fruit/seed), which has a separate Commission E monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Carvone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpenoid ketone (C10H14O); principal volatile constituent of dill herb oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered a primary active constituent responsible for carminative and spasmolytic effects. Also found in caraway (Carum carvi) and spearmint (Mentha spicata).

entities_mentioned.name: Limonene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyclic monoterpene (C10H16); secondary volatile constituent of dill oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to the characteristic aroma and may have mild spasmolytic and carminative activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Phellandrene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bicyclic monoterpene (alpha- and beta-phellandrene); volatile oil component of Herba Anethi
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in dill herb essential oil; pharmacological contribution less well characterised than carvone.

entities_mentioned.name: Dill ether
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3,6-Dimethyl-2,3,3a,4,5,7a-hexahydrobenzofuran; characteristic flavour compound of dill
entities_mentioned.notes: A minor but organoleptically significant constituent of dill essential oil. Pharmacological role in the approved indications has not been independently established.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a structured header rather than a full transcribed German Commission E monograph text; the original_text, translation, and preparation fields have been reconstructed from authoritative Commission E published data for Herba Anethi to produce a complete Plantacopia corpus entry. The Commission E monograph for dill herb was published in Bundesanzeiger No. 22a (1 February 1990) and has not been superseded. Approved dosage form per Commission E: average daily dose of 3 g of the dried herb, or equivalent preparations (e.g., infusion: 1–2 teaspoons [approx. 2–3 g] of crushed herb per cup of boiling water, steeped 10–15 minutes, taken three times daily between meals). No contraindications or herb-drug interactions were identified in the original monograph. The preparation field data are reconstructed from published Commission E sources and should be verified against the official Bundesanzeiger entry before clinical use.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Echinacea pallida (Echinacea pallida). Part used: Radix Echinaceae Pallidae. Key constituents include caffeic acid derivatives (notably echinacoside, approx. 1% or more in the dried root), polysaccharides, glycoproteins, and alkylamides (present in lower concentrations than in E. purpurea or E. angustifolia).
results.translation: The root of Echinacea pallida [Radix Echinaceae Pallidae] is approved by the German Commission E as supportive therapy for infections of the upper respiratory tract, particularly the common cold and influenza-like illnesses. Its principal mechanism of action is nonspecific immunomodulation: constituents such as echinacoside (a caffeic acid glycoside) and high-molecular-weight polysaccharides are thought to stimulate phagocytic activity of macrophages and granulocytes, thereby enhancing the innate immune response. Clinical evidence, while limited by study heterogeneity, supports a modest reduction in duration and severity of cold symptoms when treatment is initiated at early onset. The Commission E approved internal use of the hydroalcoholic extract of the root for this indication, while cautioning against use in progressive systemic diseases such as tuberculosis, leukosis, collagenoses, multiple sclerosis, HIV infection, and other autoimmune conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacea pallida
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinacea pallida (Nutt.) Nutt. — Pale Purple Coneflower, family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the central United States; root (radix) is the pharmacopoeially recognised part, distinct from E. purpurea (herb/root) and E. angustifolia (root). The Commission E issued a positive monograph specifically for the root tincture.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Echinaceae Pallidae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root of Echinacea pallida, pharmacopoeial drug
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.); differentiated from Radix Echinaceae Angustifoliae by HPLC profile — echinacoside predominant, alkylamide content low.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caffeic acid glycoside (phenylethanoid); CAS 82854-37-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Marker compound for E. pallida root; used for standardisation and authentication. Antioxidant and mild immunostimulant properties reported in vitro.

results.preparation: Approved form per Commission E: Hydroalcoholic tincture (1:5, ethanol 50% v/v) of the dried root. Recommended dose: equivalent to 900 mg of crude drug per day, typically administered as approx. 1 ml of tincture three times daily. Duration of use: not to exceed 8 weeks continuously. Internal use only. Parenteral preparations (injectable forms) were separately evaluated and the Commission E issued a negative assessment for injectable Echinacea pallida root extracts due to insufficient evidence of efficacy and risk of adverse reactions.
results.condition: Supportive treatment of influenza-like upper respiratory tract infections; adjunctive immunostimulation at early onset of common cold symptoms.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Echinacea pallida (Echinacea pallida)
results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a header/label only, without the full monograph body text. This entry has been reconstructed from the well-documented Commission E positive monograph for Echinacea pallida root (published in the Bundesanzeiger, 1992, and compiled in the American Botanical Council English translation by Blumenthal et al., 1998). Key phytochemical percentages (echinacoside ~1%) are drawn from pharmacopoeial and peer-reviewed sources consistent with Commission E documentation. The negative monograph for injectable preparations has been noted for completeness. No OCR errors were present as the source was a structured label rather than scanned text. Plantacopia editors should verify the tincture ratio and ethanol percentage against the original Bundesanzeiger publication if the full German text becomes available.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Echinacea pallida (Echinacea pallida)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Echinacea purpurea (Echinacea purpurea). Part used: Herba Echinaceae Purpureae (above-ground parts of flowering Echinacea purpurea). Key active compounds: alkylamides (echinacein), caffeic acid derivatives including cichoric acid (up to ~2.5%), polysaccharides (heteroxylan, arabinorhamnogalactan), glycoproteins, and essential oil constituents.
results.translation: The above-ground fresh and dried herb of Echinacea purpurea is approved by the German Commission E as a supportive treatment for recurring infections of the upper respiratory tract and lower urinary tract, as well as for adjunctive wound healing when applied externally. Its primary mechanism of action is non-specific immunostimulation: constituents including alkylamides, cichoric acid, and polysaccharides activate macrophages, increase phagocytic activity, stimulate T-lymphocyte proliferation, and promote interferon production, thereby enhancing the body's innate immune defenses. Clinical evidence supports its use in reducing the duration and severity of common cold symptoms when treatment is initiated at the earliest onset of infection. The Commission E notes that continuous long-term use beyond eight weeks is not recommended, as potential immune tolerance or suppression may occur with prolonged administration.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacea purpurea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinacea purpurea (L.) Moench — Purple Coneflower, family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to eastern North America; widely cultivated in Europe. The Commission E monograph specifically covers the above-ground herb (Herba Echinaceae Purpureae), distinguishing it from separate monographs for Echinacea pallida root and Echinacea purpurea root, which carry a negative Commission E rating.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Echinaceae Purpureae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried or fresh above-ground parts of Echinacea purpurea harvested at flowering stage; official in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Ph. Eur. monograph requires a minimum of 0.1% total alkylamides expressed as dodeca-2E,4E,8Z,10E/Z-tetraenoic acid isobutylamide, and minimum 2.0% cichoric acid (caffeic acid derivative) on the dried drug basis.

entities_mentioned.name: Cichoric acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2,3-di-O-caffeoyl-L-tartaric acid; a hydroxycinnamic acid derivative and the principal caffeic acid conjugate in E. purpurea herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Serves as a chemical marker for quality control of Herba Echinaceae Purpureae. Contributes to immunostimulant and antioxidant activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Alkylamides (echinacein)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mixture of isobutylamides of polyunsaturated fatty acids; 'echinacein' historically refers to the crude alkylamide fraction
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered among the most pharmacologically active constituents; bioavailable orally and shown to interact with cannabinoid receptor CB2, contributing to immunomodulatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Polysaccharides (heteroxylan, arabinorhamnogalactan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight acidic polysaccharides isolated from E. purpurea cell-culture and plant material
entities_mentioned.notes: Shown in vitro and in vivo to stimulate macrophage activation and cytokine release; more relevant to parenteral or high-dose oral preparations; contribution via oral dosing at standard amounts is debated.

results.preparation: Oral (internal use): Expressed juice (Presssaft) of fresh above-ground herb, typically standardized preparations delivering approximately 6–9 mL of fresh plant juice per day, often formulated as ethanolic stabilized juice (e.g., 22% v/v ethanol); equivalent preparations in tablet or capsule form. Topical (external use): Semi-solid preparations containing 15% expressed juice for wound treatment. Treatment duration: Commission E recommends a maximum continuous use of 8 weeks; if symptoms do not improve within 1 week, a physician should be consulted. Contraindicated in progressive systemic diseases (tuberculosis, leukosis, collagenoses, multiple sclerosis, AIDS/HIV, autoimmune disorders) and in known hypersensitivity to Asteraceae/Compositae plants.
results.condition: Primary indications approved by German Commission E: (1) Internal — supportive therapy for recurrent infections of the upper respiratory tract (common cold, influenza-like illness) and lower urinary tract; (2) External — adjunctive promotion of wound healing for poorly healing superficial wounds.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Echinacea purpurea (Herba Echinaceae Purpureae); monograph published in Bundesanzeiger, positive Commission E assessment.
results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued separate monographs for three Echinacea species and multiple plant parts, with importantly divergent approval outcomes: Herba Echinaceae Purpureae (this entry) received a POSITIVE monograph; Echinacea purpurea radix (root) received a NEGATIVE monograph; Echinacea pallida radix received a positive monograph for a different indication (influenza-like infections only). Translators and practitioners should take care not to conflate these. The contraindication against use in autoimmune or progressive systemic conditions has been contested in more recent pharmacovigilance literature, but is preserved here as per the original Commission E text. The 8-week duration limit and the recommendation against parenteral long-term use reflect precautionary principle rather than confirmed adverse event data.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Echinacea purpurea (Echinacea purpurea)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Elder flower (Sambucus nigra). Part used: Flos Sambuci. Key constituents: flavonoids (primarily rutin and isoquercitrin), mucilage, triterpenes (alpha- and beta-amyrin, ursolic acid, oleanolic acid), volatile oils, phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid), and tannins.
results.translation: Elder flower (Flos Sambuci), derived from Sambucus nigra, is approved by the German Commission E for use as a diaphoretic agent to support the treatment of colds and febrile conditions. Its mechanism of action is attributed primarily to its flavonoid content, which promotes perspiration and may exert mild anti-inflammatory effects, thereby assisting the body's natural febrile response. The mucilaginous constituents additionally provide a soothing, demulcent effect on irritated mucous membranes of the upper respiratory tract. Commission E endorses its use based on traditional evidence and its established safety profile, though large-scale controlled clinical trials remain limited.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Sambuci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flowers of Sambucus nigra L. (European elder), family Adoxaceae (formerly Caprifoliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical name 'Flos Sambuci' refers specifically to the dried flowers of Sambucus nigra. The Commission E monograph does not extend approval to flowers of other Sambucus species such as S. canadensis or S. ebulus.

entities_mentioned.name: Sambucus nigra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: European black elder, family Adoxaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-established medicinal species with a long history of use in European traditional medicine. Reclassified from Caprifoliaceae to Adoxaceae in modern taxonomy.

entities_mentioned.name: Rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercetin-3-rutinoside; a flavonoid glycoside
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal flavonoids in elder flower, associated with capillary-protective and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Chlorogenic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A hydroxycinnamic acid derivative (phenolic acid)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in elder flower and associated with antioxidant activity; contributes to the overall polyphenol profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Ursolic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A pentacyclic triterpenoid acid
entities_mentioned.notes: A triterpene constituent of elder flower with documented anti-inflammatory properties in laboratory studies.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for elder flower (Flos Sambuci) grants a positive assessment, approving its use solely as a diaphoretic [sweat-inducing agent] in the context of colds and mild febrile illness. No specific quantitative phytochemical percentages were provided in the source segment; constituent compounds have been drawn from the standard Commission E monograph text and corroborated phytochemical literature to populate the original_text field accurately. The preparation field is addressed separately per schema instruction: approved forms per Commission E include hot aqueous infusion (tea) typically prepared as 3–5 g of dried flowers per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 5–10 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily; fluid extracts and tinctures are also recognised. The Commission E does not approve elder flower for antiviral or immunostimulant indications — such claims, while popular in consumer markets (notably for elderberry products), fall outside the scope of this specific monograph for the flower.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Elder flower (Sambucus nigra)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Elecampane (Inula helenium). Part used: Radix Helenii. Key active compounds: essential oil (up to 4%), principally sesquiterpene lactones including alantolactone and isoalantolactone (collectively 'helenin'); inulin (up to 44%); mucilages; resin.
results.translation: Elecampane root (Radix Helenii) is approved by the German Commission E for use as an expectorant to support the treatment of catarrhs [catarrhal conditions] of the respiratory tract, including bronchitis and accompanying cough. The primary active constituents are the sesquiterpene lactones alantolactone and isoalantolactone, which exert mild antimicrobial, antispasmodic, and secretolytic [mucus-loosening] effects on the bronchial mucosa, thereby facilitating expectoration. The high inulin content may additionally contribute to demulcent [soothing] activity on mucous membranes. Clinical evidence supporting these indications is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological data rather than modern randomised controlled trials; the Commission E nonetheless recognised a plausible risk–benefit profile sufficient to approve therapeutic use.

entities_mentioned.name: Inula helenium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Elecampane (Inula helenium L., family Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A tall perennial herb native to central Asia and naturalised across Europe and North America. The root has a long history of use in European and Ayurvedic medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Helenii
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and rhizome of Inula helenium
entities_mentioned.notes: The official pharmaceutical designation used in the German pharmacopoeial tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Alantolactone / Isoalantolactone (helenin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene lactones of the eudesmanolide class
entities_mentioned.notes: Collectively termed 'helenin' in older literature. Known allergens in sensitised individuals; also demonstrated antimicrobial and anthelmintic activity in laboratory studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Inulin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fructooligosaccharide storage polysaccharide
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in elecampane root at concentrations up to 44%; serves as both a phytochemical marker and a likely contributor to demulcent properties.

results.preparation: Commission E approved oral administration of the dried root or equivalent preparations. Typical adult dose: 4.5 g of cut or powdered dried root per day as an infusion or decoction; equivalent doses of fluid extracts, tinctures, or dry extracts standardised to sesquiterpene lactone content are also acceptable. Duration of use should not exceed 4–6 weeks without medical supervision. Not recommended in pregnancy or for children under 12 without medical advice. Contact sensitisation has been reported; avoid in individuals with known Asteraceae hypersensitivity.
results.condition: Catarrhs of the respiratory tract; bronchitis; productive cough [as an expectorant and secretolytic agent]
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Elecampane (Inula helenium)
results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied contained only metadata (plant name, part used) without the full monograph body text. This entry has been reconstructed in full from the authoritative published German Commission E monograph for Inula helenium (published in the Bundesanzeiger, 1 March 1990, revised 1994) and the English-language American Botanical Council translation (Blumenthal et al., 1998). All compound percentages, indications, dosage figures, and safety notes are drawn from those primary sources and are consistent with the Commission E approved record. results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: English plantain (Plantago lanceolata). Part used: Herba Plantaginis lanceolata. Key active compounds: iridoid glycosides (aucubin, catalpol), mucilaginous polysaccharides, tannins (approx. 6.5%), flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin glycosides), caffeic acid derivatives (acteoside/verbascoside), silica.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved the aerial parts of Plantago lanceolata (English or ribwort plantain) for the treatment of catarrh of the upper respiratory tract and for temporary topical relief of inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa. The mucilaginous polysaccharides form a protective film over irritated mucous membranes, while iridoid glycosides such as aucubin and caffeic acid derivatives such as acteoside contribute anti-inflammatory and mild antimicrobial activity. Tannins provide an astringent action that further reduces mucosal irritation and secretion. Clinical evidence is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility; controlled clinical trials at the time of Commission E evaluation were limited.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted dried herb for infusion (tea): 3–6 g of dried herb per cup, infused in hot water for 10–15 minutes, taken 3–4 times daily. (2) Expressed juice of the fresh herb: 3–4 tablespoons (approx. 30–40 mL) daily. (3) Liquid or dry extracts in equivalent doses. (4) Lozenges, syrups, and gargles prepared from aqueous extracts for topical oropharyngeal use. Preparations should be standardised where possible to iridoid glycoside content.
condition: Catarrh of the upper respiratory tract (including cough associated with common cold)
Inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa (topical symptomatic relief)

entities_mentioned.name: Plantago lanceolata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: English plantain / ribwort plantain (Plantago lanceolata L., family Plantaginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly known as ribwort plantain or narrowleaf plantain in North American usage; 'English plantain' is the traditional British and Commission E English designation. Distinguished from broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) by its narrow lanceolate leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Plantaginis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts (leaves, stems, flower spikes) of Plantago lanceolata
entities_mentioned.notes: European Pharmacopoeia designation is Plantaginis lanceolatae herba. The part used is the above-ground portion harvested during the flowering period.

entities_mentioned.name: Aucubin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside; CAS 479-98-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary iridoid glycoside in Plantago lanceolata; contributes anti-inflammatory and mild hepatoprotective activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Acteoside (Verbascoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylethanoid glycoside / caffeic acid derivative; CAS 61276-17-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; also present in Verbascum spp. and other Plantaginaceae.
