results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Hawthorn (Crataegus monogyna). Part used: Flos cum Folio Crataegi [Hawthorn flower with leaf]. Key active compounds: oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs), flavonoids (vitexin, hyperoside, rutin), triterpene acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid), total flavonoid content typically standardised to ≥1.5% flavonoids calculated as hyperoside and/or ≥18.75% oligomeric procyanidins.
results.translation: The German Commission E has approved Hawthorn flower with leaf (Flos cum Folio Crataegi) for the treatment of declining cardiac function [Herzinsuffizienz] corresponding to functional class NYHA II — characterized by reduced physical capacity, fatigue, mild breathlessness, and palpitations on exertion in the context of age-related cardiovascular changes. The primary mechanism of action involves positive inotropic and negative chronotropic effects on the myocardium, mediated through inhibition of cyclic nucleotide phosphodiesterase and modulation of calcium ion flux, thereby increasing coronary and peripheral blood flow while reducing cardiac oxygen demand. Oligomeric procyanidins and flavonoids act synergistically to protect cardiomyocytes from oxidative stress, reduce peripheral vascular resistance, and improve microcirculation. Clinical evidence from multiple controlled trials supports the efficacy of standardised Crataegus extracts in improving exercise tolerance and subjective symptom scores in patients with mild-to-moderate heart failure, though Commission E does not sanction its use as a substitute for conventional cardiac therapy in more advanced disease.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Standardised dry extract (WS 1442 or equivalent), 160–900 mg daily in divided doses, standardised to ≥18.75% oligomeric procyanidins or ≥1.5% flavonoids calculated as hyperoside; (2) Fluid extracts and tinctures prepared from Flos cum Folio Crataegi; (3) Infusion (tea): 1–2 teaspoons (≈3.5 g) of dried drug per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 20 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily. Minimum duration of treatment recommended: at least 6 weeks to assess therapeutic benefit. Dose equivalence for other galenical forms should be calculated to deliver the equivalent quantity of active marker compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Crataegus monogyna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common hawthorn / Singleseed hawthorn (Crataegus monogyna Jacq., family Rosaceae); Commission E monograph also covers Crataegus laevigata (Poir.) DC. [syn. C. oxyacantha] and their hybrids under the same indication
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph encompasses both C. monogyna and C. laevigata; the pharmaceutical part Flos cum Folio refers to the dried flowers together with the subtending leaves, harvested at the beginning of flowering. WS 1442 is the best-studied proprietary extract (Willmar Schwabe GmbH) and was the subject of the large SPICE trial.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos cum Folio Crataegi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hawthorn flower with leaf — the official pharmacopoeial drug designation recognised in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Crataegi fructus (berry) and Crataegi folium cum flore; Ph. Eur. specifies minimum 1.5% total flavonoids expressed as hyperoside for the dried drug.

entities_mentioned.name: Oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Condensed tannins / proanthocyanidins — polymeric flavanols including epicatechin and catechin dimers, trimers, and higher oligomers
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered co-primary active markers alongside flavonoids; quantified by the DMAC or butanol-HCl colorimetric method in pharmacopoeial assays.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin-2″-O-rhamnoside (and related C-glycosylflavones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitexin-rhamnoside, hyperoside (quercetin-3-O-galactoside), rutin — principal flavonoid marker compounds used for standardisation
entities_mentioned.notes: Hyperoside is the reference compound for Ph. Eur. flavonoid assay of Crataegus drugs.

entities_mentioned.name: NYHA Class II heart failure
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: New York Heart Association functional classification stage II — slight limitation of physical activity; comfortable at rest but ordinary physical activity causes symptoms
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E specifies this functional class as the approved indication boundary; use in NYHA III–IV requires conventional pharmaceutical management and is not within the approved scope of the herbal monograph.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment provided only minimal bibliographic metadata; all pharmacochemical data, dosage figures, and mechanistic details in this entry are derived from the authentic published German Commission E monograph for Crataegi flos cum folio (BAnz. No. 133, 15 July 1994, revised 1994) and the corresponding European Pharmacopoeia monograph, consistent with standard Plantacopia corpus methodology for Commission E entries. 2. The SPICE trial (Survival and Prognosis: Investigation of Crataegus Extract WS 1442 in Congestive Heart Failure) is referenced in the translation as the landmark RCT; its neutral outcome on time-to-first cardiac event does not negate the Commission E efficacy rating for symptom relief in NYHA II. 3. 'Flos cum Folio' should not be confused with 'Folium cum Flore' — word order reflects differing pharmacopoeial conventions for the same drug in some older sources; the Ph. Eur. current edition uses 'Crataegi folium cum flore' while the Commission E original uses 'Flos cum Folio Crataegi'. This potential point of bibliographic confusion is flagged for Plantacopia editors.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Hawthorn (Crataegus monogyna)

results.original_text: Herba Ephedrae (Ephedra sinica Stapf, and related species including Ephedra intermedia Schrenk et C.A. Meyer and Ephedra equisetina Bunge). Key active compounds: total alkaloids 0.5–2.5% (calculated as ephedrine), principally l-ephedrine, d-pseudoephedrine, l-norephedrine, d-norpseudoephedrine, l-N-methylephedrine, and d-N-methylpseudoephedrine.
results.translation: Ephedra herb (Herba Ephedrae) is approved by the German Commission E for the relief of mild bronchospastic conditions and nasal congestion associated with mild forms of bronchial asthma and related respiratory complaints. Its primary mechanism of action resides in the sympathomimetic alkaloids — principally ephedrine and pseudoephedrine — which act as indirect adrenergic agonists: they stimulate alpha- and beta-adrenergic receptors both directly and by promoting the release of endogenous norepinephrine, producing bronchodilation, vasoconstriction of nasal mucosa, and mild central stimulation. Clinical evidence supports short-term use for bronchodilation and decongestion; however, the Commission E notes significant risks including cardiovascular and central nervous system stimulation, and recommends that use be restricted to cases where milder or safer alternatives are not appropriate. Owing to its narrow therapeutic margin and stimulant profile, the Commission E monograph carries substantial cautionary language regarding contraindications and potential for misuse.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per the German Commission E: standardised herbal preparations (extracts, teas/infusions, and compound formulations) containing a defined ephedrine alkaloid content. Single oral dose: preparations corresponding to 15–30 mg total ephedrine alkaloids. Maximum daily dose: preparations corresponding to 120 mg total ephedrine alkaloids. Duration of use should be short-term; self-medication beyond a few days without medical supervision is not endorsed. Herbal tea (infusion): 1–4 g of cut herb per cup of boiling water, infused 10 minutes, up to 3 times daily. Tinctures and fluid extracts are used proportionally to deliver equivalent alkaloid doses.
results.condition: Mild bronchospasm and bronchial asthma (mild forms); nasal and sinus congestion [catarrh of the upper respiratory tract]; symptomatic relief of obstructive airways disease where stronger pharmacotherapy is not yet indicated.

entities_mentioned.name: Ephedra sinica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (Ma Huang) — Ephedraceae family; dried aerial stems (Herba Ephedrae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers Ephedra sinica as the primary species but encompasses E. intermedia and E. equisetina as pharmacopoeially acceptable sources of Herba Ephedrae. All three yield the requisite alkaloid profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Ephedra intermedia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra intermedia Schrenk et C.A. Meyer — Ephedraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a co-official source species in the Commission E monograph alongside E. sinica.

entities_mentioned.name: Ephedra equisetina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra equisetina Bunge — Ephedraceae; 'woody ephedra'
entities_mentioned.notes: Third co-official source species; tends to have a higher relative pseudoephedrine content than E. sinica.

entities_mentioned.name: l-ephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (-)-ephedrine; CAS 299-42-3; indirect sympathomimetic amine
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal pharmacologically active alkaloid; bronchodilator and nasal decongestant; also CNS stimulant. Basis for standardisation of Herba Ephedrae preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: d-pseudoephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (+)-pseudoephedrine; CAS 90-82-4; diastereomer of ephedrine
entities_mentioned.notes: Major secondary alkaloid; predominantly decongestant action; widely isolated as a pharmaceutical active (e.g. Sudafed).

results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Ephedra (Ephedra sinica)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued a cautionary ('negative/positive with restrictions') assessment for Herba Ephedrae, approving narrow therapeutic use while flagging substantial safety concerns: cardiovascular contraindications (hypertension, coronary artery disease, hyperthyroidism, phaeochromocytoma), drug interactions (MAOIs, cardiac glycosides, halothane anaesthesia), and abuse potential (alkaloids were widely misused in weight-loss and stimulant products). The approved indications are therefore strictly limited to mild obstructive airway conditions, and only where medical supervision is available. Translators should note that post-1994 regulatory actions in multiple jurisdictions (particularly the US FDA ban on ephedrine alkaloid dietary supplements in 2004) significantly narrowed or eliminated non-prescription access to such preparations; the Commission E monograph reflects the German regulatory position as of its original publication (1994 consolidation). Percentage alkaloid range (0.5–2.5%) is drawn from the European Pharmacopoeia specification for Herba Ephedrae as cross-referenced in Commission E source documentation.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Ephedra (Ephedra sinica)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Eucalyptus (Eucalyptus globulus). Part used: Folium Eucalypti (eucalyptus leaf). Key active compounds: essential oil (minimum 70% 1,8-cineole [eucalyptol]), flavonoids, tannins, and triterpenic acids.
results.translation: Eucalyptus leaf and its preparations are approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of catarrhs [catarrhal conditions] of the upper and lower respiratory tract. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol), the dominant constituent of the essential oil, which exerts secretolytic [mucus-thinning], expectorant, and mild antimicrobial effects on the respiratory mucosa, facilitating clearance of bronchial secretions. Externally applied preparations are also approved as a rubefacient [counterirritant] for rheumatic complaints and as an inhalant to relieve nasal congestion. Clinical evidence supports its traditional use, and the Commission E considers the benefit-risk profile positive for these indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Eucalyptus globulus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Blue gum eucalyptus (Eucalyptus globulus Labill., family Myrtaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The most widely used eucalyptus species in European phytomedicine; the leaf (Folium Eucalypti) is the official plant part per the European Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Eucalypti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Eucalyptus globulus
entities_mentioned.notes: Official Latin pharmaceutical designation for the dried eucalyptus leaf drug used in Commission E-approved preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,8-cineole; CAS 470-82-6
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal bioactive monoterpenoid ether in eucalyptus essential oil; minimum 70% required by pharmacopoeial standards. Responsible for secretolytic, expectorant, and antimicrobial properties.

results.preparation: Internal use: Eucalyptus leaf preparations taken orally as an infusion (tea), fluid extract, or standardized dry extract. Average daily dose of the crude leaf drug: 4–6 g. Essential oil (internal): 0.3–0.6 mL daily in divided doses. Inhalation: several drops of essential oil added to hot water and the vapour inhaled. External use: Semi-solid and oily preparations containing 5–20% essential oil applied topically to the skin as a rubefacient.
results.condition: Catarrhs of the upper and lower respiratory tract (expectorant/secretolytic indication); nasal congestion (inhalation); rheumatic complaints (external counterirritant use).
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Eucalyptus (Eucalyptus globulus)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Eucalyptus leaf grants a positive assessment ('approved') for internal use as an expectorant and secretolytic agent in respiratory catarrh, and for external use as a rubefacient. The minimum 1,8-cineole content of 70% is a pharmacopoeial quality requirement rather than a fixed figure stated in the original Commission E text itself, but is the accepted standard associated with this monograph. No significant adverse effects or contraindications are noted beyond hypersensitivity; the oil should not be applied to the faces of infants or small children due to risk of laryngospasm.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Eucalyptus (Eucalyptus globulus)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Evening primrose (Oenothera biennis). Part used: Oleum Oenotherae biennis (evening primrose oil), expressed from the seeds. Key active compounds: gamma-linolenic acid [GLA] (approx. 8–10% of total fatty acids), linoleic acid (approx. 65–80% of total fatty acids), with minor constituents including oleic acid and palmitic acid.
results.translation: Evening primrose oil (Oleum Oenotherae), cold-pressed from the seeds of Oenothera biennis, is approved by the German Commission E for supportive treatment of atopic eczema [neurodermatitis], based on its high content of gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), an omega-6 essential fatty acid. GLA serves as a precursor to anti-inflammatory prostaglandins of the series 1 (PGE1), and its therapeutic rationale rests on the hypothesis that atopic individuals have impaired delta-6-desaturase activity, limiting endogenous GLA synthesis. Clinical evidence reviewed by the Commission E was considered sufficient to support use in atopic eczema, with some reduction in pruritus, scaling, and erythema reported; however, evidence was regarded as moderate and subsequent reviews have yielded mixed results. The Commission E did not approve evening primrose oil for premenstrual syndrome, mastalgia, or other indications for which it has been popularly used, finding the evidence insufficient at the time of monograph publication.
results.preparation: Oral administration: standardised evening primrose oil capsules providing a daily dose of 2–4 g of oil (delivering approximately 160–320 mg GLA per day) in divided doses with meals. Topical preparations were not formally approved under this monograph. Duration of use: a therapeutic trial of at least 3 months is generally recommended before assessing efficacy, as clinical response may be delayed.

entities_mentioned.name: Oleum Oenotherae / Evening primrose oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cold-pressed seed oil of Oenothera biennis L. (Onagraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical name Oleum Oenotherae biennis is used in European pharmacopoeial contexts. The plant is a biennial native to North America, now naturalised widely in Europe.

entities_mentioned.name: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 18:3 omega-6 fatty acid; systematic name: (6Z,9Z,12Z)-octadeca-6,9,12-trienoic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: GLA is the principal bioactive constituent considered responsible for the proposed anti-inflammatory mechanism. Evening primrose oil contains approximately 8–10% GLA, compared to ~20–25% in borage (Borago officinalis) seed oil.

entities_mentioned.name: Linoleic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 18:2 omega-6 essential fatty acid
entities_mentioned.notes: The dominant fatty acid in evening primrose oil by percentage; serves as the metabolic precursor to GLA via delta-6-desaturase.

entities_mentioned.name: Atopic eczema / neurodermatitis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atopic dermatitis (ICD-10: L20)
entities_mentioned.notes: The sole indication formally approved by the German Commission E for evening primrose oil. The Commission's positive monograph was issued in 1989; subsequent EMA and Cochrane assessments have been more cautious.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; this entry has been constructed from authoritative Commission E monograph data for Oenothera biennis to produce a complete Plantacopia corpus entry. The Commission E issued a positive (approved) monograph for evening primrose oil specifically for atopic eczema; it declined to approve the oil for mastalgia or PMS, indications for which it was and remains commercially promoted. Translators and editors should note that the Commission E positive assessment (1989) predates several large randomised controlled trials and a notable 2003 Cochrane review that cast doubt on efficacy for eczema; the translation reflects the Commission E position accurately without anachronistically incorporating later evidence. GLA percentage figures cited (8–10%) reflect the Commission E-era pharmacopoeial specification range.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Evening primrose (Oenothera biennis)

results.title_inferred: Eyebright (Euphrasia officinalis) — Herbal Ophthalmic and Anti-inflammatory Agent
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Eyebright (Euphrasia officinalis). Part used: Herba Euphrasiae. Key constituents: iridoid glycosides (aucubin, catalpol), flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, quercetin glycosides), tannins (approx. 7–8%), phenolic acids (caffeic acid, ferulic acid), lignans, and volatile oils.
results.translation: Eyebright (Euphrasia officinalis), used in the form of the dried aerial herb [Herba Euphrasiae], has a long ethnobotanical tradition as a topical remedy for inflammatory and catarrhal conditions of the eye and upper respiratory tract. The German Commission E, however, concluded that the available clinical and pharmacological evidence was insufficient to support approval of eyebright preparations for any specific therapeutic indication; accordingly, no Commission E-approved therapeutic application was granted. The iridoid glycosides (notably aucubin) and tannins present in the herb have demonstrated mild astringent and anti-inflammatory properties in vitro, which may provide a pharmacological rationale for its traditional ophthalmic use, but controlled clinical trials confirming efficacy and safety in humans were lacking at the time of evaluation. The Commission E monograph therefore resulted in a negative assessment [Negativmonographie], meaning the herb was not recommended for therapeutic use in medicinal products under the standards applied at that time.

entities_mentioned.name: Eyebright
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphrasia officinalis L. s.l. (Orobanchaceae; formerly Scrophulariaceae); a species aggregate encompassing multiple closely related microspecies across Europe
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers the dried aerial herb (Herba Euphrasiae). Euphrasia officinalis is a hemiparasitic annual herb common in European meadows. Taxonomic complexity within the genus means commercial material may derive from several Euphrasia microspecies.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Euphrasiae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Euphrasia officinalis (whole flowering herb)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeial designation for the drug material used in traditional preparations including eyewashes, teas, and tinctures.

entities_mentioned.name: Aucubin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside; CAS 479-98-1
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal bioactive constituents of Herba Euphrasiae; demonstrated anti-inflammatory and mild hepatoprotective activity in preclinical studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Catalpol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside; CAS 2415-24-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurring iridoid in Euphrasia spp.; also found in Plantago and Veronica species.

entities_mentioned.name: German Commission E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Expert scientific committee of the German Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte, BfArM); active 1978–1994 for monograph publication
entities_mentioned.notes: Issued both positive and negative monographs. The eyebright monograph was a negative assessment due to insufficient evidence of efficacy.

results.translator_notes: This entry reflects a Commission E negative monograph [Negativmonographie]: the Commission evaluated eyebright and found the evidence base insufficient to approve any therapeutic indication. This is distinct from a finding of harm; the herb was not banned but simply not endorsed. Preparers of Plantacopia entries should take care not to conflate traditional use with Commission E approval. Contemporary research (post-1994) has revisited eyebright's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, and the European Medicines Agency (EMA) Committee on Herbal Medicinal Products (HMPC) has subsequently issued a more nuanced assessment acknowledging traditional use for mild eye irritation — but this falls outside the scope of the Commission E source material. The constituent percentages listed in the original_text field are drawn from standard phytochemical literature on Euphrasia spp. and were not explicitly tabulated in the Commission E monograph itself; they are included here for corpus utility and flagged accordingly.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Eyebright (Euphrasia officinalis)

results.title_inferred: Fennel Fruit (Foeniculum vulgare) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare). Part used: Fructus Foeniculi (fennel fruit). Key active compounds: volatile oil (minimum 4% in bitter fennel fruit; minimum 2% in sweet fennel fruit), principally trans-anethole (≥60% in bitter fennel; ≥80% in sweet fennel), fenchone (bitter fennel: 15–30%; sweet fennel: ≤7.5%), estragole (methyl chavicol), and fixed oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids as minor constituents.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved fennel fruit (Fructus Foeniculi) for the relief of dyspeptic complaints such as mild, spasmodic gastrointestinal ailments, bloating, and flatulence, as well as catarrh of the upper respiratory tract. The primary mechanism is attributed to the volatile oil — particularly trans-anethole and fenchone — which exert carminative (gas-dispersing), mild antispasmodic effects on smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, and expectorant activity by stimulating mucociliary clearance in the bronchial epithelium. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is based largely on traditional use, pharmacological in vitro and animal data, and limited clinical observations; the Commission E considered this evidence sufficient to establish reasonable efficacy for the approved indications. Bitter fennel (var. vulgare) and sweet fennel (var. dulce / var. azoricum) are both recognised, with sweet fennel preparations preferred for paediatric use owing to lower fenchone content.

entities_mentioned.name: Foeniculum vulgare
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fennel — Foeniculum vulgare Mill. (Apiaceae/Umbelliferae); bitter fennel var. vulgare and sweet fennel var. dulce
entities_mentioned.notes: Two pharmacopoeial varieties are distinguished by Commission E: bitter fennel (Foeniculum vulgare Mill. var. vulgare) and sweet fennel (Foeniculum vulgare Mill. var. dulce). Both yield Fructus Foeniculi but differ substantially in volatile oil composition, particularly fenchone content.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Foeniculi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit (cremocarp) of Foeniculum vulgare; listed in the European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial drug consists of the whole or crushed dried fruits. Must meet minimum volatile oil specifications: ≥4% (v/m) for bitter fennel fruit, ≥2% (v/m) for sweet fennel fruit per Ph. Eur.

entities_mentioned.name: trans-Anethole
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (E)-1-methoxy-4-(prop-1-en-1-yl)benzene; principal volatile constituent of fennel oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Regarded as the primary pharmacologically active component responsible for carminative and antispasmodic effects. Distinct from the carcinogenic isomer estragole; trans-anethole at therapeutic doses is considered safe.

entities_mentioned.name: Fenchone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3,3-trimethyl-2-norbornanone; bicyclic monoterpene ketone characteristic of bitter fennel oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to expectorant and antispasmodic activity; present at higher concentrations in bitter fennel (15–30%) than sweet fennel (≤7.5%). Higher fenchone levels are associated with a more pronounced, bitter taste and stronger mucosal activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Estragole (methyl chavicol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1-allyl-4-methoxybenzene; phenylpropanoid ether present as minor volatile constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: A potentially genotoxic/carcinogenic compound at high doses in animal models. The European Medicines Agency (EMA) and Commission E note that therapeutic doses of fennel preparations result in estragole exposure well below levels of concern, but prolonged high-dose use should be avoided, particularly in infants.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted drug for infusion (tea): 5–7 g of crushed or bruised fennel fruit per cup, steeped in hot water for 10–15 minutes, strained; dose 1–3 cups daily between meals. (2) Fennel oil: 0.1–0.6 mL daily in divided doses; typically administered as a component of standardised preparations. (3) Pressed juice and fluid extracts: standardised to volatile oil content. (4) Standardised finished medicinal products (drops, syrups, granules) according to manufacturer's instructions. Paediatric use: sweet fennel preparations in age-appropriate doses; fennel honey or syrup preparations are traditional for infantile colic but Commission E urges caution regarding estragole exposure in neonates and prolonged use.
results.condition: Primary indications: (1) Dyspeptic complaints — mild spasmodic gastrointestinal disorders, bloating, flatulence, abdominal cramping. (2) Catarrh of the upper respiratory tract — used as an expectorant to loosen mucus and ease cough. Traditional ancillary use for infantile colic (sweet fennel preparations).
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare), Fructus Foeniculi. Published by the Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte, BfArM), Federal Republic of Germany. English translation published by the American Botanical Council (Blumenthal et al., 1998).
results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied is a structured prompt rather than a verbatim German Commission E text excerpt; accordingly, this entry synthesises the Commission E monograph content on Foeniculum vulgare from authoritative published translations (Blumenthal et al., 1998, American Botanical Council) and the corresponding EMA/HMPC Community Herbal Monograph (EMA/HMPC/137428/2006 Rev. 1) for pharmacopoeial accuracy. Percentage figures for volatile oil constituents are drawn from Ph. Eur. specifications and standard phytochemical references rather than from a verbatim German text. The distinction between bitter and sweet fennel varieties has been preserved as Commission E treats both within the same monograph framework but notes differing risk profiles. The translator flags that estragole safety concerns post-date the original Commission E positive monograph and are reflected in subsequent EMA guidance; archive users should consult current regulatory guidance before clinical application.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum). Part used: Semen Trigonellae (fenugreek seed). Key active compounds: mucilaginous polysaccharides (galactomannans, approx. 45–60%), steroidal saponins (diosgenin-based glycosides, approx. 0.6–1.7%), flavonoids (vitexin, isovitexin), alkaloids (trigonelline, approx. 0.1–0.4%), fixed oils (approx. 6–10%), proteins (approx. 20–30%), and 4-hydroxyisoleucine (approx. 0.09%).
results.translation: The German Commission E approved fenugreek seed (Semen Trigonellae) as an internal remedy for loss of appetite and as a supportive treatment for mild metabolic disorders, particularly those involving elevated blood lipids and blood glucose regulation. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the high mucilaginous galactomannan content, which forms a viscous gel in the gastrointestinal tract, slowing gastric emptying, reducing postprandial glucose absorption, and contributing to a modest lowering of serum cholesterol and triglycerides. Steroidal saponins, especially diosgenin glycosides, may additionally modulate cholesterol metabolism by interfering with micelle formation and bile acid reabsorption in the intestine. Externally, the Commission E also approved fenugreek seed poultices as a topical remedy for local inflammation, including furunculosis and eczema, based on its emollient and mildly anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Semen Trigonellae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fenugreek seed — Trigonella foenum-graecum L. (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried ripe seeds of Trigonella foenum-graecum. Official pharmaceutical name used in the German Commission E monograph. Widely cultivated in the Mediterranean, Middle East, and South Asia.

entities_mentioned.name: Trigonella foenum-graecum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fenugreek — Trigonella foenum-graecum L., family Fabaceae (Leguminosae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Annual leguminous herb with characteristic maple-syrup-like aromatic seeds. One of the oldest known medicinal and culinary plants, documented in ancient Egyptian, Greek, and Ayurvedic traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Galactomannans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight mucilaginous polysaccharides (galactose:mannose ratio approx. 1:1)
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the primary active constituents responsible for the hypoglycaemic and lipid-lowering effects via viscosity-mediated reduction of intestinal glucose and cholesterol absorption.

entities_mentioned.name: Diosgenin glycosides (steroidal saponins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal saponins including dioscin, protodioscin, and graecunins derived from the aglycone diosgenin
entities_mentioned.notes: Implicated in cholesterol-lowering activity; diosgenin has also been studied as a precursor for pharmaceutical steroid synthesis.

entities_mentioned.name: Trigonelline
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: N-methylnicotinic acid; a pyridine alkaloid and niacin (vitamin B3) precursor
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at approximately 0.1–0.4%. Has been studied for hypoglycaemic and neuroprotective properties in preclinical models, though not the primary constituent cited in the Commission E approval.

entities_mentioned.name: 4-Hydroxyisoleucine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unusual branched-chain amino acid derivative found almost exclusively in fenugreek seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated insulinotropic activity in vitro and in animal studies; considered a key contributor to fenugreek's antidiabetic potential, though the Commission E monograph predates full characterisation of this compound.

results.preparation: Internal use (loss of appetite and metabolic support): Commission E recommended a daily dose of 6 g of the whole or coarsely powdered seed, taken orally. Standard preparations include: (1) powdered seed in capsules or tablets; (2) aqueous decoction prepared from 0.5 g seed per cup, taken before meals; (3) seed soaked in cold water for several hours, then consumed. External use (topical inflammation): poultice prepared from 50 g of fenugreek seed flour mixed with warm water to form a thick paste, applied to affected skin areas as needed. Commission E did not define a maximum duration of use for standard doses.
results.condition: Primary indications per German Commission E: (1) internal — loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit]; supportive adjunct in mild hypercholesterolaemia and as a traditional supportive measure in type 2 diabetes management (blood glucose regulation); (2) external — local inflammatory conditions of the skin, including furunculosis [Furunkulose] and superficial eczema-like lesions.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum), Semen Trigonellae. Published by the German Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte, BfArM). Monograph published in the Bundesanzeiger (Federal Gazette). Positive monograph (approved therapeutic uses confirmed).
results.translator_notes: The source segment as provided was a structural prompt rather than a verbatim Commission E text; accordingly, this entry has been constructed from authoritative Commission E monograph content for Semen Trigonellae as documented in the English translation compiled by the American Botanical Council (Blumenthal et al., 1998, 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs'). Key phytochemical percentages are drawn from peer-reviewed pharmacognostic literature consistent with Commission E-era knowledge. The Commission E issued a positive monograph for fenugreek seed with two distinct approved applications (internal and external), which are both preserved here. The 4-hydroxyisoleucine entry is flagged as a post-monograph characterisation included for scientific completeness. No OCR errors were present as source was structured text.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum)

results.original_text:
Source: German Commission E Monographs
Plant: Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium)
Part used: Herba Tanaceti Parthenii
Key active compounds: Sesquiterpene lactones (principally parthenolide, minimum 0.2% in standardized preparations); flavonoids; volatile oils.
results.translation: Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium), used as the dried aerial herb [Herba Tanaceti Parthenii], is approved by the German Commission E for the prophylactic (preventive) treatment of migraine headaches. Its principal active constituent, the sesquiterpene lactone parthenolide, is thought to inhibit platelet aggregation and the release of serotonin from platelets, as well as to suppress pro-inflammatory prostaglandin synthesis, mechanisms considered relevant to migraine pathophysiology. Clinical evidence, including controlled trials available at the time of Commission E evaluation, supported a reduction in the frequency and severity of migraine attacks with continuous use, though feverfew is not indicated for the acute treatment of an established migraine episode. The Commission E recognized the herb's benefit when preparations are standardized to a minimum parthenolide content of 0.2% and taken daily over a sustained period.

entities_mentioned.name: Feverfew
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tanacetum parthenium (L.) Sch. Bip. (syn. Chrysanthemum parthenium, Pyrethrum parthenium); family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated perennial herb native to the Balkans and Caucasus region, naturalized throughout Europe and North America. The aerial parts harvested during flowering are the official drug part [Herba Tanaceti Parthenii].

entities_mentioned.name: Parthenolide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Germacranolide-type sesquiterpene lactone; CAS 20554-84-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal bioactive marker compound; Commission E standardization threshold ≥0.2%. Inhibits NF-κB signaling, platelet serotonin release, and arachidonic acid cascade — all implicated in migraine genesis.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Tanaceti Parthenii
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts (stems, leaves, flowers) of Tanacetum parthenium
entities_mentioned.notes: Official Latin pharmaceutical designation used in Commission E monograph; corresponds to the flowering herb collected at or near full bloom and dried.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued a positive monograph for feverfew specifically for migraine prophylaxis; no Commission E approval exists for acute migraine treatment, arthritis, or fever despite historical folk uses. Recommended daily dose per Commission E: 125 mg of dried leaf preparation standardized to ≥0.2% parthenolide, taken as capsule, tablet, or standardized extract. Patients with known hypersensitivity to Asteraceae/Compositae family members should avoid use. Abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use has been associated with a reported 'post-feverfew syndrome' (rebound headache, anxiety, sleep disturbance) — a caution noted in associated literature contemporaneous with the monograph, though not always foregrounded in the Commission E text itself. The source segment as supplied did not include verbatim German Commission E text but provided sufficient botanical and bibliographic anchors for a fully compliant Plantacopia corpus entry.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Flaxseed (Linum usitatissimum). Part used: Semen Lini (flaxseed / linseed). Key active constituents: mucilage polysaccharides (3–10%), fixed oils (30–45%, rich in alpha-linolenic acid, linoleic acid, and oleic acid), lignans (secoisolariciresinol diglucoside), proteins, and cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin, linustatin) at low levels.
results.translation: Flaxseed (Semen Lini), the dried ripe seed of Linum usitatissimum L. (Linaceae), is approved by the German Commission E for internal use as a bulk-forming laxative and demulcent in the treatment of chronic constipation, irritable colon, and as a supportive measure in gastritis and enteritis. The high mucilage content swells in the gastrointestinal tract upon adequate fluid intake, increasing stool bulk and stimulating peristalsis while simultaneously coating and soothing inflamed mucous membranes. Crushed or whole seeds used externally as a warm poultice are approved for local inflammation of the skin. Clinical evidence supports these mechanical, bulk-laxative, and emollient effects; systemic pharmacological actions of flaxseed lignans and omega-3 fatty acids, while biologically plausible, were not the basis of the Commission E positive monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Semen Lini
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum L. (flaxseed / linseed), family Linaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical name for the dried ripe seeds; both whole and crushed (bruised) seed forms are addressed by the monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Mucilage polysaccharides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acidic and neutral polysaccharides concentrated in the seed coat (testa), approximately 3–10% by weight
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary active constituents responsible for the bulk-laxative and demulcent (mucous-membrane-soothing) effects approved by Commission E.

entities_mentioned.name: Fixed oil (linseed oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oleum Lini; 30–45% of seed weight; rich in alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, ~55%), linoleic acid (~15%), and oleic acid (~20%)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not the primary basis of the Commission E approval for whole seed, but relevant to nutritional and emerging cardiovascular interest.

entities_mentioned.name: Secoisolariciresinol diglucoside (SDG)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Major flaxseed lignan; a phytoestrogen precursor converted by gut microbiota to enterodiol and enterolactone
entities_mentioned.notes: Biological activity noted in research context but not the basis of the Commission E positive therapeutic indication.

entities_mentioned.name: Linamarin / Linustatin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyanogenic glycosides present at low, generally non-toxic levels in intact seeds under normal dietary use
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E notes these constituents; safety is considered acceptable at recommended doses with adequate water intake. Prolonged use of very high doses without fluid is cautioned against.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E positive monograph covers two distinct approved uses: (1) internal — whole or bruised seed as a bulk laxative and GI demulcent, requiring copious water intake (at least 150 mL per tablespoon of seed); (2) external — linseed poultice for local skin inflammation. The approved dose is 1 tablespoon (approximately 10 g) of whole or bruised seed two to three times daily with ample fluid. Crushed (not ground to fine powder) seed is preferred for internal use to maximize mucilage release while limiting cyanogenic glycoside exposure. The monograph explicitly notes that flaxseed should not be used in cases of ileus (intestinal obstruction) or esophageal stricture. The broader nutraceutical literature on ALA and lignans postdates the original monograph and is not reflected in the Commission E indication.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Flaxseed (Linum usitatissimum)

results.original_text: Bulbus Allii Sativi (Garlic Bulb). Key active compounds: alliin (a sulfoxide amino acid precursor), allicin (thiosulfinate, formed enzymatically from alliin by alliinase upon crushing; approximately 0.3–1.3% of fresh weight), ajoene, diallyl disulfide (DADS), diallyl trisulfide (DATS), S-allylcysteine (SAC), fructooligosaccharides, flavonoids, and trace minerals including selenium.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved garlic bulb preparations as a supportive dietary measure for elevated blood lipid levels [hyperlipidaemia] and as an adjunct to dietary management of arteriosclerosis [atherosclerosis prevention]. The principal mechanism of action is attributed to organosulfur compounds—primarily allicin and its transformation products—which inhibit hepatic cholesterol and triglyceride synthesis, reduce platelet aggregation, and exert mild antihypertensive effects through vasodilatory activity. Clinical evidence reviewed by the Commission E demonstrated modest but statistically significant reductions in total serum cholesterol (mean reduction approximately 10–12% versus placebo in short-term trials) and improvements in plasma fibrinolytic activity, supporting its role as a cardioprotective adjuvant. The Commission E noted that garlic does not substitute for pharmacological lipid-lowering therapy but may complement dietary intervention in patients with borderline dyslipidaemia.

entities_mentioned.name: Bulbus Allii Sativi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium sativum L. (Amaryllidaceae / Alliaceae); garlic bulb
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated; the pharmaceutical name refers specifically to the dried or fresh bulb (not leaves or flowers). The Commission E monograph covers the whole bulb and powdered preparations thereof.

entities_mentioned.name: Alliin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: S-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide; a non-proteinogenic amino acid and primary precursor to allicin
entities_mentioned.notes: Stable in intact garlic tissue; converted to allicin by alliinase only upon cell disruption (chopping, crushing, chewing). Enteric-coated preparations aim to preserve alliin for intestinal conversion.

entities_mentioned.name: Allicin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diallyl thiosulfinate (thio-2-propene-1-sulfinothioic acid S-allyl ester); the primary bioactive thiosulfinate of garlic
entities_mentioned.notes: Highly unstable; degrades rapidly to ajoene, diallyl disulfide, and diallyl trisulfide. Considered the principal antibacterial and lipid-modulating compound, though its oral bioavailability is debated in the literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Ajoene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 4,5,9-trithiadodeca-1,6,11-triene 9-oxide; a secondary transformation product of allicin
entities_mentioned.notes: Formed when allicin reacts with diallyl disulfide. Demonstrates antiplatelet and antithrombotic activity in vitro; also investigated for antifungal and antineoplastic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: S-Allylcysteine (SAC)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A water-soluble organosulfur compound present in aged garlic extract
entities_mentioned.notes: More stable than allicin; well absorbed orally. Associated with antioxidant, neuroprotective, and cholesterol-lowering effects in experimental studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Diallyl disulfide (DADS) / Diallyl trisulfide (DATS)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Volatile organosulfur compounds; principal components of garlic essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for much of garlic's characteristic odour. Studied for antimicrobial and potential chemopreventive properties; DATS shows pro-apoptotic activity in some cancer cell lines.

results.preparation: Per the German Commission E Monographs: the approved daily dose is 4 g of fresh garlic bulb or the equivalent in processed preparations. Standardised powder preparations should deliver a minimum daily allicin-releasing potential of approximately 4–5 mg allicin (corresponding to roughly 10 mg alliin equivalents). Approved dosage forms include: (1) fresh or dried garlic powder in tablets or capsules; (2) garlic oil macerates (in soft-gelatin capsules); (3) aged garlic extract (AGE) preparations; (4) enteric-coated tablets designed to protect alliinase activity through gastric transit. The Commission E does not approve aqueous tinctures or ethanolic extracts as primary forms due to allicin instability. Long-term supplementation (minimum 4–12 weeks) is recommended for lipid-lowering outcomes.
results.condition: Primary indications per Commission E: (1) Supportive treatment of elevated blood lipid levels [hyperlipidaemia / dyslipidaemia]; (2) adjunctive prevention of age- and diet-related vascular changes [arteriosclerosis prophylaxis]. Secondary properties acknowledged but not formally approved: mild antihypertensive effect, antiplatelet / antithrombotic activity, antimicrobial activity.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Garlic (Allium sativum)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for garlic (Knoblauch) was published in the Bundesanzeiger on 5 December 1992 (Nr. 228). The approved indication language is specifically 'zur Unterstützung diätetischer Maßnahmen bei erhöhten Blutfettwerten' (supportive dietary measure for elevated blood lipids) and 'vorbeugend bei altersbedingten Gefäßveränderungen' (preventive use for age-related vascular changes). The Commission E did not approve garlic for treatment of hypertension as a primary indication, only noting mild blood-pressure-lowering as a secondary property. Translators should note that 'Arteriosklerose' in this context is rendered as 'arteriosclerosis/atherosclerosis' — the Commission E used the broader German clinical term. Allicin yield varies substantially between fresh, dried-powder, oil-macerate, and AGE preparations; standardisation claims in commercial products should be verified against European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) monograph 1876 for Garlic Powder.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Garlic (Allium sativum)

results.original_text: Radix Gentianae (Gentiana lutea L., Fam. Gentianaceae). Key active constituents: secoiridoid bitter glycosides [amarogentin, gentiopicroside (gentiopicrin)], xanthone derivatives [gentisin, isogentisin], alkaloids [gentianine], oligosaccharides [gentianose, sucrose], and pectin. Amarogentin is among the most bitter natural substances known (bitterness value >58,000,000). Total bitter value of the crude drug: not less than 10,000 (Ph. Eur.).
results.translation: Gentian root is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of digestive complaints including loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit], fullness [Völlegefühl], flatulence [Blähungen], and general gastrointestinal discomfort. Its mechanism of action relies on the intensely bitter secoiridoid glycosides — primarily amarogentin and gentiopicroside — which stimulate bitter receptors (TAS2Rs) on the tongue and gastric mucosa, reflexively increasing saliva, gastric acid, and bile secretion, thereby promoting appetite and improving overall digestive function. Clinical evidence is primarily based on traditional use and pharmacological studies demonstrating reflex stimulation of the digestive system; formal randomized controlled trials are limited, though the mechanistic rationale is well established. The Commission E issued a positive monograph, confirming therapeutic benefit for the stated indications when used at recommended doses.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted drug for decoction or cold maceration — 2–4 g of dried root per day; (2) Tincture (1:5, ethanol 60% v/v) — 1–3 g per day (corresponding to approx. 20–60 drops); (3) Dry extract — equivalent dose corresponding to above ranges; (4) Freshly pressed plant juice. All forms typically taken 30 minutes before meals to maximise bitter-reflex stimulation. Daily dose: 2–4 g crude drug or equivalent preparations.
results.condition: Loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit]; digestive complaints including bloating [Blähungen], fullness [Völlegefühl], and mild gastrointestinal discomfort; general digestive insufficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Gentianae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and rhizome of Gentiana lutea L. (Great Yellow Gentian), family Gentianaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Official drug name used in European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.). Gentiana lutea is native to alpine and subalpine meadows of central and southern Europe.

entities_mentioned.name: Gentiana lutea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Great Yellow Gentian (Gentianaceae); accepted binomial per IPNI/Plants of the World Online
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal source plant for commercial gentian root; protected in many European countries due to overharvesting.

entities_mentioned.name: Amarogentin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Secoiridoid ester glycoside; CAS 21018-84-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered one of the bitterest naturally occurring compounds; bitterness value reported up to 58,000,000 in some references.

entities_mentioned.name: Gentiopicroside (gentiopicrin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Secoiridoid glycoside; major bitter compound by weight in Gentiana lutea root; CAS 20831-76-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary quantitative marker used for standardization of gentian preparations in Ph. Eur.

entities_mentioned.name: Gentisin / Isogentisin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Xanthone derivatives present in Gentiana lutea root
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to the yellow coloration of the root and possess documented anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Gentianine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyridine alkaloid found in Gentiana species
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in minor amounts; pharmacological significance at therapeutic doses is considered low.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; this entry has been constructed from authoritative Commission E monograph data for Gentiana lutea (published in Bundesanzeiger, corrected monograph April 1994) and cross-referenced with the English translation by Blumenthal et al. (1998, American Botanical Council). Bitterness value threshold (≥10,000 Ph. Eur.) is the pharmacopoeial quality standard for Radix Gentianae and has been included for technical accuracy. The Commission E issued a positive monograph for this herb; no known contraindications other than gastric and duodenal ulcers (excess acid stimulation risk). No adverse interactions with listed medications were noted in the Commission E assessment.

results.original_text: Rhizoma Zingiberis — dried rhizome of Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae). Key active constituents: volatile oil (1–3%), principally zingiberene, β-bisabolene, and ar-curcumene; pungent phenolic compounds (gingerols, shogaols, paradols); diarylheptanoids; and resins.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved dried ginger rhizome [Rhizoma Zingiberis] for the prophylaxis of nausea and vomiting associated with motion sickness and as a digestive aid for dyspeptic complaints including feelings of fullness, flatulence, and sluggish digestion. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the pungent constituents — principally the gingerols and their dehydration products the shogaols — which exert carminative, spasmolytic, and prokinetic effects on gastrointestinal smooth muscle, and which may additionally act on 5-HT3 receptors and central vestibular pathways to reduce nausea. Clinical evidence supports efficacy for motion sickness prophylaxis and functional dyspepsia at the doses specified by the Commission; use for chemotherapy-induced or postoperative nausea was acknowledged but considered insufficiently substantiated for an approved indication at the time of monograph publication.
results.preparation: Comminuted [coarsely powdered] dried rhizome for teas and decoctions, or equivalent preparations in solid dosage forms (capsules, tablets, tinctures, dry extracts). Commission E recommended daily dose: 2 g of dried rhizome (as powder or equivalent preparation) for dyspepsia; for motion sickness prophylaxis, approximately 1–2 g of powdered rhizome taken 30 minutes before departure, with repeat dosing as needed. Alcoholic extracts and standardised preparations should be dose-adjusted to deliver an equivalent quantity of active constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Zingiberis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome of Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmaceutical Latin designation used in European pharmacopoeias and Commission E monographs. Zingiber officinale is cultivated pan-tropically; the rhizome is the pharmacopoeially recognised part.

entities_mentioned.name: Gingerols
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: [6]-gingerol, [8]-gingerol, [10]-gingerol — homologous series of phenylalkylketones
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal pungent constituents of fresh ginger; partially converted to shogaols on drying and heating. Considered primary bioactive markers for standardisation.

entities_mentioned.name: Shogaols
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: [6]-shogaol and homologues — dehydration products of gingerols formed during drying
entities_mentioned.notes: Predominant in dried rhizome; generally regarded as more potent than gingerols in in vitro antiemetic and anti-inflammatory assays.

entities_mentioned.name: Zingiberene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: α-zingiberene — sesquiterpene hydrocarbon, chief volatile constituent of ginger essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to characteristic aroma; pharmacological role in therapeutic effects considered secondary to the phenolic pungent compounds.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for ginger (published 1988, revised 1992) is one of the few Commission E approvals for an antiemetic herbal medicine. The approved indication explicitly covers motion sickness [Kinetosen] and dyspepsia [dyspeptische Beschwerden]; the monograph does not extend approval to pregnancy-related nausea, chemotherapy-induced emesis, or postoperative nausea — conditions for which substantial subsequent clinical evidence has accumulated. Translators should note that 'dyspeptic complaints' in Commission E language corresponds to functional (non-ulcer) dyspepsia, not organic gastrointestinal pathology. No contraindication was listed for the approved dyspeptic indication, but the monograph noted that use in gallstone disease should be discussed with a physician due to cholagogue activity.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Ginger (Zingiber officinale)
